topics.title: Characteristic Tests on Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Tests on Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristic Tests on Induction Motors** are a set of standardized no-load and loaded electrical tests performed on a three-phase (or single-phase) squirrel-cage / slip-ring induction motor to indirectly determine its equivalent-circuit parameters, compute its performance metrics (efficiency $\eta$, slip $s$, power factor $\cos\phi$, torque $T$), and plot the complete **performance characteristics** without loading the machine up to its full rated mechanical output.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPEL508)**, this experiment is mapped to **CO2 – Characterize electrical prime movers used to drive centrifugal pumps** and is typically evaluated as a **14-mark ESE module question** with sub-parts on circuit analysis and graphical plotting.

The three canonical tests are:

1. **No-Load Test (Running Light Test)** — conducted at rated voltage, no shaft load.
2. **Blocked-Rotor Test (Locked-Rotor / Short-Circuit Test)** — rotor physically prevented from rotating.
3. **Load Test (Direct Mechanical Loading)** — motor coupled to a brake-drum / eddy-current / pony-brake dynamometer.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Doctor's Stethoscope" of an Electric Motor**
>
> Imagine you are a doctor examining a patient. You cannot cut them open, so you use non-invasive tools — a stethoscope, blood-pressure cuff, ECG — to infer what is happening *inside*. The induction motor is a sealed, rotating machine; you cannot open it while running. The **no-load test** is like a "resting ECG" (it reveals the iron / friction losses and magnetizing current). The **blocked-rotor test** is like a "stress test with the body held still" (it reveals the copper losses and leakage reactances). The **load test** is like a "treadmill test" (it reveals how the motor behaves under real working conditions).
>
> Combining the three gives a complete "health card" of the motor — its **efficiency curve, torque–speed curve, current–load curve, power-factor curve, and slip curve**.

### 1.3 Standard Test Metrics & Boundary Conditions

- **Rated Voltage** (line-to-line, e.g., **415 V**, **50 Hz**) is applied during no-load.
- **Reduced Voltage** (typically **20–30 % of rated**) is applied during blocked-rotor.
- **Ambient reference temperature** is taken as **25 °C**; winding resistance is corrected to **75 °C** as per IS 325 / IEEE 112.
- The motor is **star-connected** unless otherwise specified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The lab record is expected to include (i) tabulated observations, (ii) computed equivalent-circuit parameters referred to stator, (iii) **circle diagram** (if applicable for slip-ring machines), and (iv) plotted performance curves.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are These Tests "Indirect"?

A direct measurement of rotor copper loss, core loss, and leakage reactance inside a spinning motor is physically impossible without intrusive instrumentation. Engineers therefore use **two-port network theory** — they excite the motor from the stator terminals under two extreme boundary conditions (open-circuit-like at no-load, and short-circuit-like at blocked rotor) and **back-calculate** the equivalent-circuit parameters.

The **per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator** of an induction motor is:

- $R_1$ → stator winding resistance per phase
- $R_2'$ → rotor winding resistance per phase, referred to stator
- $X_1$ → stator leakage reactance per phase
- $X_2'$ → rotor leakage reactance per phase, referred to stator
- $X_m$ → magnetizing reactance per phase
- $R_c$ (or $R_0$) → core-loss resistance representing iron losses
- $s$ → slip (per unit)

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Test

#### 2.2.1 No-Load Test (Running Light)

- **How:** Motor is run at rated voltage, no shaft load. Two-wattmeter method (or three-wattmeter) measures input power $P_{NL}$ and per-phase no-load current $I_{0}$.
- **Why:** At no-load, slip $s \approx 0$, so rotor branch is effectively open. Input power equals **stator core loss + friction & windage loss + a tiny stator copper loss at $I_0$**.
- **Output obtained:** $R_c$, $X_m$, rotational losses $P_{rot}$.

#### 2.2.2 Blocked-Rotor Test (Locked Rotor)

- **How:** Rotor is mechanically locked. Reduced voltage is applied (just enough to circulate **rated current**). Input power $P_{BR}$ and current $I_{BR}$ are measured.
- **Why:** At $s = 1$, the rotor branch impedance is much smaller than the magnetizing branch, so the motor essentially behaves like a **short-circuited transformer**. Input power is dissipated almost entirely as copper loss in stator and rotor (referred).
- **Output obtained:** $R_{01} = R_1 + R_2'$ and $X_{01} = X_1 + X_2'$.

#### 2.2.3 Load Test

- **How:** Motor is coupled to a **pony brake / rope-brake / eddy-current dynamometer**. Load is incremented in steps; readings of line current, power input, speed (tachometer), and spring-balance readings are taken.
- **Why:** Gives **real operating data** — actual slip, mechanical output torque, efficiency, and power factor as a function of shaft load.
- **Output obtained:** Plotted performance curves.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas use **per-phase** quantities unless otherwise noted. Use `\vert` notation in written work to avoid table-breakage in mark-down renderers.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Test Source |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_{NL}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_{0L}}$ | — | No-load |
| 2 | No-load copper loss (stator) | $P_{Cu1,NL} = 3 \, I_{0ph}^{\,2} \, R_1$ | W | No-load |
| 3 | Constant rotational + core loss | $P_{const} = P_{NL} - P_{Cu1,NL}$ | W | No-load |
| 4 | Core-loss resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{ph}^{\,2}}{P_{const}/3} = \dfrac{3 \, V_{ph}^{\,2}}{P_{const}}$ | $\Omega$ | No-load |
| 5 | No-load reactive power | $Q_{NL} = 3 \, V_{ph}^{\,2} \left(\dfrac{1}{X_m}\right)$ ; $Q_{NL} = \sqrt{(S_{NL})^2 - (P_{NL})^2}$ | VAR | No-load |
| 6 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_m = \dfrac{3 \, V_{ph}^{\,2}}{Q_{NL}}$ | $\Omega$ | No-load |
| 7 | Blocked-rotor power factor | $\cos\phi_{BR} = \dfrac{P_{BR}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_{BR,L} \, I_{BR,L}}$ | — | Blocked rotor |
| 8 | Equivalent impedance (per phase) | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{BR,ph}}{I_{BR,ph}}$ | $\Omega$ | Blocked rotor |
| 9 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = Z_{01}\cos\phi_{BR} = \dfrac{P_{BR}}{3 \, I_{BR,ph}^{\,2}}$ | $\Omega$ | Blocked rotor |
| 10 | Equivalent leakage reactance | $X_{01} = Z_{01}\sin\phi_{BR} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Blocked rotor |
| 11 | Approximate split (standard assumption) | $R_1 \approx R_2'$ and $X_1 \approx X_2'$ | $\Omega$ | Convention |
| 12 | Rotor copper loss at slip $s$ | $P_{Cu2} = s \, \dfrac{P_{g2}}{1} = s \, P_{ag}$ where $P_{g2} = \dfrac{P_{m}}{1-s}$ | W | Theory |
| 13 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{m}}{1-s} = T_{ag} \, \omega_s$ | W | Theory |
| 14 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1-s) \, P_{ag}$ | W | Theory |
| 15 | Shaft output (after friction) | $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot}$ | W | Load test |
| 16 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | p.u. | Load test |
| 17 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm | Theory |
| 18 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{60 \, P_{ag}}{2\pi N_s}$ | N·m | Theory |
| 19 | Shaft torque (brake test) | $T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \, R_{brake} \, g$ (SI: $\times 9.81$) | N·m | Load test |
| 20 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \,\%$ | % | Load test |
| 21 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L}$ | — | Load test |
| 22 | Percentage slip | $\% s = s \times 100$ | % | Load test |
| 23 | Starting torque (approx.) | $T_{st} \approx \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^{\,2} \, R_2' / s}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}$ with $s=1$ | N·m | Theory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These three tests form the backbone of every motor manufacturer's type-test report (per IS 325, IEEE 112, IEC 60034). They allow a motor to be "rated" without destroying it, and the equivalent-circuit parameters feed directly into Variable-Frequency Drive (VFD) commissioning software (e.g., ABB DriveSize, Siemens SIZER).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Slip, Air-Gap Power, and Rotor Copper Loss

Consider a 3-phase induction motor with $P$ poles, supply frequency $f$ Hz, rotor speed $N_r$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed (rotating field speed).**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of slip (per unit).**

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Step 3 — Rotor frequency.** The relative speed between rotating field and rotor is $(N_s - N_r)$. Hence the frequency of rotor-induced EMF is:

$$f_r = s \, f$$

**Step 4 — Air-gap power.** Air-gap power is the total power crossing the air gap from stator to rotor:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \, I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 5 — Power division inside the rotor circuit.** Rotor copper loss (per phase) is $I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, R_2'$; mechanical power developed is $I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, R_2' \left(\dfrac{1-s}{s}\right)$.

Summing: $I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, R_2' + I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, R_2' \left(\dfrac{1-s}{s}\right) = I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$ per phase, confirming the power balance.

**Step 6 — Rotor copper loss in terms of $P_{ag}$.**

$$P_{Cu2} = s \, P_{ag}$$

**Step 7 — Mechanical power developed (gross).**

$$P_m = (1-s) \, P_{ag}$$

**Step 8 — Shaft output (after rotational losses).**

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Separation of No-Load Losses

At no-load, $s \approx 0$ and rotor branch is effectively open. Therefore **rotor copper loss is negligible** and total no-load input power $P_{NL}$ equals:

$$P_{NL} = P_{Cu1,NL} + P_{core} + P_{F\&W} = 3 \, I_{0}^{\,2} \, R_1 + P_{const}$$

Solving for the combined constant loss (core + friction + windage):

$$P_{const} = P_{NL} - 3 \, I_{0}^{\,2} \, R_1 \quad \text{(W)}$$

Once $P_{const}$ is known, the core-loss resistance is:

$$R_c = \dfrac{3 \, V_{ph}^{\,2}}{P_{const}} \quad (\Omega)$$

And the no-load reactive power is:

$$Q_{NL} = \sqrt{S_{NL}^{\,2} - P_{NL}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_{0L} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}$$

Leading to:

$$X_m = \dfrac{3 \, V_{ph}^{\,2}}{Q_{NL}} \quad (\Omega)$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Blocked-Rotor Equivalent Impedance

At blocked rotor, $s = 1$ and the rotor branch resistance $R_2'/s = R_2'$ is very small compared to $X_m$ and $R_c$. Hence the magnetizing branch is **negligibly shunted**, and the motor reduces to a series $R$–$X$ circuit:

$$Z_{01} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2')$$

**Step 1 — Per-phase voltage:**

$$V_{BR,ph} = \dfrac{V_{BR,L}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Per-phase impedance magnitude:**

$$Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{BR,ph}}{I_{BR,ph}}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent resistance (from input watts):**

$$R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{BR}}{3 \, I_{BR,ph}^{\,2}}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent leakage reactance:**

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{\,2} - R_{01}^{\,2}}$$

**Step 5 — Approximate split (standard KTU assumption):**

$$R_1 = R_2' = \dfrac{R_{01}}{2}, \qquad X_1 = X_2' = \dfrac{X_{01}}{2}$$

For a more accurate split, the stator resistance $R_1$ is found by **direct DC resistance measurement** and then $R_2' = R_{01} - R_1$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Test | $s$ | Equivalent Circuit Looks Like | Main Quantity Extracted |
|------|-----|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **No-Load** | $\approx 0$ | Rotor branch open; magnetizing branch active | $R_c$, $X_m$, $P_{rot}$ |
| **Blocked-Rotor** | $= 1$ | Magnetizing branch inactive; rotor branch in series with stator | $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$ |
| **Load** | variable | Full circuit | $P_{out}$, $\eta$, $\cos\phi$, $s$, torque |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style 7-mark sub-part)

> **Given:** 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V, star-connected induction motor.
> - **No-load test:** $V_L = 415$ V, $I_{0L} = 6.5$ A, $P_{NL} = 380$ W, $R_1$ (DC per phase) = $0.6\ \Omega$.
> - **Blocked-rotor test:** $V_{BR,L} = 110$ V, $I_{BR,L} = 22$ A, $P_{BR} = 1700$ W.

**Find (a)** magnetizing reactance and core-loss resistance, **(b)** rotor referred resistance and leakage reactances, **(c)** starting torque.

---

**(a) Magnetizing branch parameters**

Per-phase no-load voltage:

$$V_{ph} = \dfrac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \text{ V}$$

Per-phase no-load current (star):

$$I_{0,ph} = 6.5 \text{ A}$$

No-load copper loss in stator:

$$P_{Cu1,NL} = 3 \times (6.5)^2 \times 0.6 = 3 \times 42.25 \times 0.6 = 76.05 \text{ W}$$

Constant rotational + core loss:

$$P_{const} = 380 - 76.05 = 303.95 \text{ W}$$

Core-loss resistance:

$$R_c = \dfrac{3 \times (239.6)^2}{303.95} = \dfrac{3 \times 57408.16}{303.95} = \dfrac{172224.5}{303.95} \approx 566.6 \ \Omega$$

Apparent no-load power:

$$S_{NL} = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 6.5 = 4673.6 \text{ VA}$$

Reactive power:

$$Q_{NL} = \sqrt{4673.6^{\,2} - 380^{\,2}} = \sqrt{21842543 - 144400} = \sqrt{21698143} \approx 4658.1 \text{ VAR}$$

Magnetizing reactance:

$$X_m = \dfrac{3 \times (239.6)^2}{4658.1} = \dfrac{172224.5}{4658.1} \approx 36.97 \ \Omega$$

---

**(b) Rotor and leakage parameters**

Per-phase blocked-rotor voltage:

$$V_{BR,ph} = \dfrac{110}{\sqrt{3}} = 63.51 \text{ V}$$

Per-phase blocked-rotor current:

$$I_{BR,ph} = 22 \text{ A}$$

Equivalent impedance:

$$Z_{01} = \dfrac{63.51}{22} = 2.887 \ \Omega$$

Equivalent resistance:

$$R_{01} = \dfrac{1700}{3 \times (22)^2} = \dfrac{1700}{1452} = 1.171 \ \Omega$$

Equivalent leakage reactance:

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{(2.887)^2 - (1.171)^2} = \sqrt{8.335 - 1.371} = \sqrt{6.964} = 2.639 \ \Omega$$

Standard split (assuming $R_1 = R_2'$ and $X_1 = X_2'$):

$$R_1 = R_2' = \dfrac{1.171}{2} = 0.586 \ \Omega \quad (\text{note: }R_1 \text{ from DC is }0.6\ \Omega,\ \text{close enough})$$

$$X_1 = X_2' = \dfrac{2.639}{2} = 1.320 \ \Omega$$

---

**(c) Starting torque**

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

Synchronous angular speed:

$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Starting torque ($s = 1$):

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^{\,2} \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

Numerator:

$$3 \times (239.6)^2 \times 0.586 = 3 \times 57408.16 \times 0.586 = 100919.2$$

Denominator:

$$\omega_s \times \left[(1.172)^2 + (2.640)^2\right] = 157.08 \times [1.3736 + 6.9696] = 157.08 \times 8.3432 = 1310.5$$

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{100919.2}{1310.5} \approx 77.01 \text{ N·m}$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation 4: Efficiency from Equivalent Circuit

For any slip $s$, the mechanical power developed is:

$$P_m = (1-s) \times P_{ag} = (1-s) \times 3 I_2^{\,\prime 2} \, \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$$

Shaft output (after subtracting $P_{rot}$):

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot}$$

Stator input power:

$$P_{in} = P_{Cu1} + P_{core} + P_{ag} = 3 I_1^{\,2} R_1 + P_{const} + P_{ag}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \,\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Test Sequence & Parameter Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: 3 Phase IM on Test Bench] --> B[Measure Stator DC Resistance R1]
    B --> C[Perform No Load Test]
    B --> D[Perform Blocked Rotor Test]
    B --> E[Perform Load Test with Brake]
    C --> F[Compute R_c and X_m from P_NL and I_0]
    D --> G[Compute R_01 and X_01 from P_BR and I_BR]
    F --> H[Equivalent Circuit Parameters]
    G --> H
    E --> I[Tabulate: V, I, P_in, N_r, W1, W2]
    I --> J[Plot Performance Curves]
    J --> K[Output: Slip, Torque, Efficiency, PF, Current vs Load]
    H --> L[Calculate Starting and Pull out Torque]
    L --> M[Final Motor Performance Report]
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit (Per Phase, Referred to Stator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[V_ph] --> R1A[R1]
    R1A --> X1A[jX1]
    X1A --> A[Node A: Air Gap]
    A --> PAR[Parallel Branch]
    PAR --> RC[R_c: Core Loss Branch]
    PAR --> XM[jX_m: Magnetizing Branch]
    A --> R2S[R2 / s: Rotor Referred]
    R2S --> X2S[jX2]
    X2S --> END[Return to Source]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — No-Load Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUPPLY[3 Phase 415 V 50 Hz Supply] --> MCB[MCCB / TP Switch]
    MCB --> CONTACTOR[Contactor with Overload]
    CONTACTOR --> V_METER[Voltmeter V_L]
    CONTACTOR --> A_METER[Ammeter I_0]
    CONTACTOR --> W_METERS[Two Wattmeter Method P_NL]
    CONTACTOR --> MOTOR[Induction Motor Stator]
    MOTOR --> FREE[Free Shaft No Load]
    W_METERS --> COMP[Compute R_c and X_m]
    V_METER --> COMP
    A_METER --> COMP
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Blocked Rotor Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUPPLY[3 Phase Variac Reduced Voltage] --> V_METER[Voltmeter V_BR]
    V_METER --> A_METER[Ammeter I_BR]
    A_METER --> W_METERS[Two Wattmeter Method P_BR]
    W_METERS --> MOTOR[Induction Motor with Rotor Locked]
    MOTOR --> LOCK[Mechanical Lock or Bolt on Shaft]
    W_METERS --> COMP[Compute R_01 and X_01]
    V_METER --> COMP
    A_METER --> COMP
    COMP --> SPLIT[Split R and X to Stator and Rotor Halves]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUPPLY[3 Phase Rated Supply] --> STAR_DELTA[Starter DOL or Star Delta]
    STAR_DELTA --> M[Induction Motor]
    M --> COUPLING[Flexible Coupling]
    COUPLING --> DYNAMO[Eddy Current or Pony Brake Dynamometer]
    DYNAMO --> SPRING_BAL[Spring Balance 1 and 2 on Lever Arm]
    DYNAMO --> TACHOMETER[Tachometer on Shaft]
    M --> METER[Voltmeter Ammeter Wattmeter]
    METER --> PLOT[Plot Performance Curves]
    SPRING_BAL --> PLOT
    TACHOMETER --> PLOT
    PLOT --> REPORT[Shaft Torque Slip Efficiency PF]
```

### 4.6 Typical Performance Curves (Qualitative Trend Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Output Power P_out] --> Y1[Slip: Rises Linearly]
    X --> Y2[Line Current: Rises after Initial Dip]
    X --> Y3[Power Factor: Improves with Load]
    X --> Y4[Efficiency: Rises Peaks then Falls]
    X --> Y5[Speed: Drops Slightly Linearly]
    X --> Y6[Slip RPM: Rises Linearly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Charts:** Each box represents a physical block on the lab bench or a derived quantity. The arrows show signal/power/measurement flow. In your record, label every block with the **SI unit** of the quantity and the **instrument** used.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the blocked-rotor test performed at reduced voltage, and how is the rated current ensured?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The blocked-rotor test is performed at **reduced voltage** (typically 20–30 % of rated) because at full voltage the short-circuit current would be 5–7 times the rated current, causing dangerous overheating of the windings before stable readings can be taken. The applied voltage is gradually increased using a **three-phase variac** until the ammeter reads the **rated full-load current** of the motor. The corresponding voltage $V_{BR}$, current $I_{BR}$, and input power $P_{BR}$ are then recorded. (3 marks — full credit for mentioning variac, rated current, and 5–7× starting current risk.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define slip of an induction motor and state its typical value at full load for a standard cage motor.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Slip $s$ is defined as the **difference between synchronous speed $N_s$ and actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$**:
>
> $$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
>
> For a standard squirrel-cage induction motor at **full load**, slip typically lies in the range **2 % to 5 %** (i.e., $s = 0.02$ to $0.05$). For large motors it is closer to 1 %; for small fractional-horsepower motors it can be up to 8 %. (3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for numerical range with units.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose marks by (i) forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor when converting line-to-phase quantities, (ii) mixing star and delta equivalent values, (iii) **omitting the temperature correction** to 75 °C when reporting $R_2'$, and (iv) confusing $P_{const}$ (which includes both core AND rotational losses) with core loss alone. Always state which losses you are computing.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V, star-connected squirrel-cage induction motor gave the following test results:
- **No-load:** 400 V, 8 A, 600 W
- **Blocked-rotor:** 200 V, 40 A, 4500 W

DC resistance measured between two stator terminals is **0.5 $\Omega$**.

Calculate:
1. The **no-load power factor** and **rotational + core loss** (assume stator copper loss is negligible at no-load for this sub-part).
2. The **magnetizing reactance $X_m$ and core-loss resistance $R_c$** per phase.
3. The **rotor resistance referred to stator $R_2'$ and total leakage reactance $X_{01}$** per phase.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the parameters obtained, calculate the **starting torque** of the motor. Also explain qualitatively, with a sketch, how the **torque–slip characteristic** would change if a **slip-ring (wound-rotor) construction** with external rotor resistance is used.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (1) No-load power factor and losses**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{600}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 8} = \dfrac{600}{5542.6} = 0.1083$$

Since stator copper loss is to be neglected at no-load:

$$P_{const} = 600 \text{ W} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**(2) Magnetizing branch**

Per-phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 400 / \sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V

Apparent power: $S_{NL} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 8 = 5542.6$ VA

Reactive power:

$$Q_{NL} = \sqrt{5542.6^{\,2} - 600^{\,2}} = \sqrt{30720423 - 360000} = \sqrt{30360423} = 5509.94 \text{ VAR} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Core-loss resistance:

$$R_c = \dfrac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{600} = \dfrac{160000}{600} = 266.67 \ \Omega \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Magnetizing reactance:

$$X_m = \dfrac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{5509.94} = \dfrac{160000}{5509.94} = 29.04 \ \Omega \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**(3) Rotor and leakage parameters**

Per-phase $V_{BR} = 200/\sqrt{3} = 115.47$ V, $I_{BR} = 40$ A

$$Z_{01} = \dfrac{115.47}{40} = 2.887 \ \Omega$$

$$R_{01} = \dfrac{4500}{3 \times 1600} = \dfrac{4500}{4800} = 0.9375 \ \Omega$$

Stator resistance per phase (DC, star):

$$R_1 = 0.5 / 2 = 0.25 \ \Omega$$

$$R_2' = R_{01} - R_1 = 0.9375 - 0.25 = 0.6875 \ \Omega \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{2.887^{\,2} - 0.9375^{\,2}} = \sqrt{8.335 - 0.879} = \sqrt{7.456} = 2.731 \ \Omega \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**(b) Starting torque**

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm} \Rightarrow \omega_s = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \times \dfrac{V_{ph}^{\,2} R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + X_{01}^{\,2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{3}{104.72} \times \dfrac{(230.94)^2 \times 0.6875}{(0.9375)^2 + (2.731)^2}$$

$$= 0.02864 \times \dfrac{36668.6}{0.879 + 7.458} = 0.02864 \times \dfrac{36668.6}{8.337}$$

$$= 0.02864 \times 4398.2 = 125.97 \text{ N·m} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

**Qualitative effect of slip-ring rotor with external resistance (4 marks):**

Adding external rotor resistance $R_{ext}$ increases the effective rotor branch resistance from $R_2'/s$ to $(R_2' + R_{ext})/s$ at any slip. This has two effects:

- The **starting torque increases** (the torque-slip curve "stretches" rightward at the start), reaching its **maximum** when the total rotor resistance equals the total leakage reactance (i.e., $R_2' + R_{ext} = X_{01}$).
- The **starting current decreases** because the rotor branch is now more resistive.
- However, the **maximum (pull-out) torque value remains the same**; only the slip at which it occurs shifts.

A sketch of the family of torque–slip curves (with progressively higher $R_{ext}$) showing the rightward shift of the peak and the increasing starting torque is required for full credit.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor is rated at **7.5 kW, 400 V, star-connected**. The following data are obtained:

- **No-load test** (at rated voltage): $I_0 = 5$ A, $P_{NL} = 250$ W.
- **Blocked-rotor test** (at rated current $I_{BR} = 18$ A): $V_{BR} = 95$ V, $P_{BR} = 1100$ W.
- **Stator DC resistance per phase** = $0.4\ \Omega$.

Determine the **equivalent circuit parameters per phase** referred to the stator: $R_c$, $X_m$, $R_1$, $R_2'$, $X_1$, $X_2'$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A load test on the same motor at rated voltage yielded the data below. **Plot the performance curves** qualitatively and determine the **full-load efficiency, power factor, and slip**.

| $I_L$ (A) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $N_r$ (rpm) | Spring balance $(W_1 - W_2)$ (kg) | Brake-drum radius (m) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
| 12        | 5500         | 1440        | 2.1                               | 0.2                    |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Equivalent circuit parameters**

$V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V, $I_0 = 5$ A, $P_{NL} = 250$ W.

$$P_{Cu1,NL} = 3 \times 5^2 \times 0.4 = 30 \text{ W} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$P_{const} = 250 - 30 = 220 \text{ W} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$R_c = \dfrac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{220} = 727.3 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$S_{NL} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 5 = 3464.1 \text{ VA}$$

$$Q_{NL} = \sqrt{3464.1^{\,2} - 250^{\,2}} = 3454.9 \text{ VAR} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$X_m = \dfrac{3 \times 230.94^2}{3454.9} = 46.32 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

For the blocked-rotor test:

$$V_{BR,ph} = 95/\sqrt{3} = 54.85 \text{ V}, \quad I_{BR,ph} = 18 \text{ A}$$

$$Z_{01} = 54.85 / 18 = 3.047 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$R_{01} = \dfrac{1100}{3 \times 18^2} = \dfrac{1100}{972} = 1.132 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{3.047^2 - 1.132^2} = \sqrt{9.285 - 1.281} = \sqrt{8.004} = 2.829 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

Since $R_1 = 0.4\ \Omega$ from DC measurement:

$$R_2' = 1.132 - 0.4 = 0.732 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

Standard split for $X$:

$$X_1 = X_2' = 2.829/2 = 1.4145 \ \Omega \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

**Summary:** $R_c = 727.3\ \Omega$, $X_m = 46.32\ \Omega$, $R_1 = 0.4\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.732\ \Omega$, $X_1 = X_2' = 1.4145\ \Omega$.

---

**(b) Full-load performance from load test**

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

Slip:

$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ or } 4\% \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Shaft torque (using spring balance readings, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \times R \times g = 2.1 \times 0.2 \times 9.81 = 4.12 \text{ N·m} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Output power:

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 4.12}{60} = 621.2 \text{ W} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Input power (given): $P_{in} = 5500$ W

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \dfrac{621.2}{5500} \times 100 = 11.3\% \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The very low efficiency at this small load indicates the motor is heavily under-loaded. For 7.5 kW rated, full load should give $P_{out} \approx 7.5$ kW.

Power factor:

$$\cos\phi = \dfrac{5500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 12} = \dfrac{5500}{8313.8} = 0.6616 \text{ lagging} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Qualitative curves (2 marks):** Six curves must be drawn on the X-axis = output power (or % load): slip (rises linearly from 0 to ~5 %), line current (rises monotonically), power factor (low at no-load, rises, peaks near full load, falls slightly), efficiency (rises, peaks at ~75 % load, then falls), speed (drops slightly), and torque (rises almost linearly).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three tests, three parameter sets** — No-load gives **magnetizing branch** ($R_c$, $X_m$, $P_{rot}$); Blocked-rotor gives **leakage branch** ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$); Load test gives **operating performance** ($s$, $\eta$, $\cos\phi$, $T$).
- **Slip** is the **fundamental variable**; always compute it first: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.
- **Per-phase conversions** — In star: $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_L$. In delta: $V_{ph} = V_L$, $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$.
- **Two-wattmeter method** — $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$ (algebraic sum). The **power factor** is obtained from $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} (W_1 - W_2)/(W_1 + W_2)$.
- **Constant losses** $P_{const}$ include both **core loss and friction & windage loss** — they are separated only by a **variable-voltage no-load test** plotting $P_{NL}$ vs $V^2$.
- **Temperature correction** — For $R_2'$, correct to 75 °C using $R_{75} = R_{cold} \times (235 + 75)/(235 + T_{ambient})$ for copper.
- **Standard split assumption** — $R_1 = R_2'$, $X_1 = X_2'$ unless $R_1$ is measured by DC; in that case $R_2' = R_{01} - R_1$.
- **Starting torque formula** — Use $s = 1$; substitute measured $R_2'$ and $X_{01}$.
- **Performance curves** — Always plot against **shaft output (kW)** or **% of full load**; slip is in **%**, efficiency in **%**, power factor is a **dimensionless ratio**.
- **Circle diagram** (KTU focus for slip-ring machines) — A 14-mark question may ask you to draw the **Heyland / Circle diagram** and identify the operating point, output line, and torque line. Remember: power line is parallel to output line; torque line passes through origin.
- **DOL starting current** is 5–7× rated; this is why star-delta starters are used in practice.
- **Lab Viva Quickies**:
  - "Why star connection during no-load test?" → To reduce line voltage per phase and limit $I_0$.
  - "Why reduced voltage during blocked-rotor?" → To avoid overheating at short-circuit.
  - "Why is no-load slip not exactly zero?" → Small positive slip is needed to overcome friction.
  - "What is the Thevenin equivalent used for?" → To simplify the equivalent circuit and derive torque equation more easily.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** "**$P_{const}$** from no-load, **$R_{01}$ & $X_{01}$** from blocked rotor, **$\eta$ & $\cos\phi$** from load test — **draw the three curves, label the axes, state the units, and you have full marks**."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Tests on DC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Tests on DC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: CHARACTERISTIC TESTS ON DC MOTORS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Characteristic tests on a DC Motor are standardized laboratory procedures performed to determine the functional relationship between key electrical and mechanical variables — namely **Armature Current (I_a)**, **Field Current (I_f)**, **Speed (N)**, **Back EMF (E_b)**, and **Torque (T)** — under varying load conditions, in order to assess the motor's suitability for a given industrial application.*

In the KTU Board Examination context, "characteristic tests" refers specifically to the **load test (or brake test)** conducted on a DC motor (shunt, series, or compound) to plot:
1. **Speed (N) vs Armature Current (I_a)**
2. **Torque (T) vs Armature Current (I_a)**
3. **Speed (N) vs Output Power (P_out)**
4. **Efficiency (η) vs Output Power (P_out)**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Fuel Economy vs Speed" Analogy (Automotive Context):**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car and plotting **Kilometers per Liter (kmpl)** against **Speed (km/h)** and **Engine Torque (Nm)**. A car engine has a "sweet spot" where efficiency peaks. Similarly, every DC motor has a unique personality chart — a signature that tells the engineer whether the motor is happy cruising at constant speed (shunt), pulling heavy loads from rest like a locomotive (series), or doing a balanced hybrid task (compound).
>
> The **characteristics test** is simply plotting these "moods" of the motor as we gradually increase the mechanical load on its shaft.

### Why This Test Matters in KTU Labs
- The course **PCPEL508 (Pumps and Prime Movers Lab)** requires the student to validate the difference between theoretical and practical performance of a prime mover.
- DC motors are frequently used as **prime movers for centrifugal pumps, blowers, and laboratory test rigs**.
- The characteristic curves directly influence **pump affinity laws** selection in subsequent experiments.

### Key Physical Quantities Involved

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Range (Lab Motor) |
|--------|----------|---------|---------------------------|
| **V** | Supply Voltage | **V (Volts)** | 220 V / 440 V DC |
| **I_a** | Armature Current | **A (Amperes)** | 2 A – 25 A |
| **I_f** | Field Current | **A (Amperes)** | 0.5 A – 1.5 A |
| **N** | Rotational Speed | **rpm** | 1000 – 1500 rpm |
| **T** | Shaft Torque | **N·m** | 5 N·m – 30 N·m |
| **E_b** | Back EMF | **V (Volts)** | 200 V – 430 V |
| **φ** | Flux per pole | **Wb (Weber)** | 0.01 – 0.05 Wb |
| **R_a** | Armature Resistance | **Ω (Ohms)** | 0.5 – 2 Ω |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations of a DC Motor

A DC motor converts **electrical energy** to **mechanical energy** based on two fundamental electromagnetic principles:

### Principle 1: The Motor Action (Faraday's Law)
When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, a force is exerted on it.

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

This force produces a **torque** that rotates the armature:

$$T_a = k_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

### Principle 2: The Generator Action (Lenz's Law)
A rotating armature in a magnetic field generates a voltage that **opposes** the applied voltage (Back EMF / Counter EMF):

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \phi \cdot N \quad \text{[V]}$$

## 2.2 The Three Sacred Equations (Most Repeated in KTU Papers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Holy Trinity" of DC Motor Equations — memorize these for full marks:**

**Equation 1 — Voltage Equation (KVL of Armature Circuit):**

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

**Equation 2 — Back EMF / Speed Relation:**

$$N = \frac{E_b}{k \cdot \phi} = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \cdot \phi}$$

**Equation 3 — Electromagnetic Torque:**

$$T_a = k \cdot \phi \cdot I_a \quad \text{(where k is machine constant)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the table below — it covers 95% of numerical problems in KTU Board Exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL equation | V |
| 2 | $E_b = \dfrac{V \cdot N_2}{N_1}$ (no-load) | Back EMF proportional to speed | V |
| 3 | $N \propto \dfrac{E_b}{\phi}$ | Speed equation (constant V) | rpm |
| 4 | $T_{shaft} = (F_1 - F_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81$ | Torque from rope-brake dynamometer | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{shaft}}{60}$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 6 | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Electrical input power | W |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Efficiency (always in %) | % |
| 8 | $P_{cu(a)} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | W |
| 9 | $P_{cu(f)} = V \cdot I_f$ | Field copper loss (shunt) | W |
| 10 | $T_a = T_{shaft} + T_{losses}$ | Torque balance equation | N·m |
| 11 | $S_R = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | Speed regulation (%) | % |
| 12 | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ (Series, $\phi \propto I_a$) | Series motor speed law | rpm |

## 2.4 Types of DC Motors & Their Distinctive Curves

### (a) DC Shunt Motor
- Field winding connected in **parallel** with armature.
- $I_f = V / R_{sh}$ is **practically constant** → hence $\phi$ is **constant** (ignoring armature reaction).
- $N \propto E_b$ → drops slightly with load due to $I_a R_a$ drop.
- **Curves:** Speed drops ~5–15% from no-load to full-load (good regulation).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Shunt = "**S**teady" speed → nearly constant, used for **lathes, fans, pumps, line shafts**.

### (b) DC Series Motor
- Field winding in **series** with armature → $I_f = I_a$.
- $\phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation) → hence $N \propto 1/I_a$ (inverse relation).
- Torque $T \propto \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ (very high starting torque).
- **Danger:** Cannot be started on no-load (runaway speed).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Series = "**S**tarting **S**uperstar" → huge torque, used for **cranes, traction, hoists, starter motors**.

### (c) DC Compound Motor
- Has **both** shunt and series field windings.
- **Cumulative Compound:** Series flux **aids** shunt flux → high starting torque + moderate speed regulation.
- **Differential Compound:** Series flux **opposes** shunt flux → poor starting torque, rarely used.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Motor Type | Pump/Prime Mover Application |
|------------|------------------------------|
| **DC Shunt** | Centrifugal pumps, blowers, conveyors (constant speed) |
| **DC Series** | Crane hoists, elevators, traction (high starting torque) |
| **DC Compound** | Presses, shears, elevators (mixed load duty) |

The characteristic curves help a **pump engineer** select the right prime mover so the **pump curve (H vs Q)** and the **system curve** intersect at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Back EMF Equation ($E_b$)

**Statement:** Show that the back EMF generated in a DC motor armature is $E_b = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$.

### Step-by-Step Proof:

Let:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in armature (A = P for lap, A = 2 for wave)
- $N$ = speed in rpm

**Step 1 — Flux cut by one conductor per revolution:**
Each conductor cuts the flux $P \cdot \phi$ once per revolution (passes under P poles).

**Step 2 — Flux cut by one conductor per minute:**
Since the armature makes $N$ revolutions per minute, flux cut per conductor per minute is:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per minute} = P \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — EMF induced per conductor (Faraday's Law):**
Average EMF per conductor = flux cut per second (divide by 60):

$$e_{conductor} = \frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

**Step 4 — Number of conductors in series per parallel path:**
The $Z$ conductors are divided into $A$ parallel paths, each with $Z/A$ conductors in series.

**Step 5 — Total back EMF (sum of series conductors in one path):**

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}}$$

For a **wave-wound** machine, $A = 2$:

$$E_b = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{120}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Equation ($T_a$)

**Statement:** Prove that the electromagnetic torque developed in a DC motor armature is $T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$.

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1 — Work done by one conductor per revolution:**
The force on one conductor is $F = B \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L$ (where $I_{cond}$ is current per conductor).

Distance traveled per revolution = circumference of armature path = $\pi D$ (where $D$ is armature diameter).

Mechanical work done per conductor per revolution:

$$W_{conductor} = F \cdot \pi D = B \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L \cdot \pi D$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of flux:**
Total flux per pole = $\phi = B \cdot A_{pole}$. For a cylindrical armature, the work done by all $Z$ conductors in one revolution equals the total flux cut:

$$W_{total} = \phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{cond} \cdot (\text{number of revolutions})$$

**Step 3 — Current per conductor:**
Total armature current $I_a$ is divided into $A$ parallel paths:

$$I_{cond} = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Total work done per revolution:**

$$W_{revolution} = \phi \cdot Z \cdot \frac{I_a}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Torque (Work done per revolution / angular distance per revolution):**
Work done per revolution = $T_a \cdot 2\pi$:

$$T_a \cdot 2\pi = \phi \cdot Z \cdot \frac{I_a}{A}$$

$$\boxed{T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{A} = 0.1591 \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{A \cdot P}}$$

The torque developed is therefore **proportional to flux and armature current**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed vs Armature Current for DC Series Motor

**Step 1:** From voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) \quad \text{(series motor)}$$

**Step 2:** Speed equation:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi}$$

**Step 3:** Before magnetic saturation, $\phi \propto I_a$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k' I_a}$$

**Step 4:** This is the famous **inverse (hyperbolic) speed curve** of a DC series motor.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBLEM:** A 220 V DC shunt motor takes an armature current of 30 A at a speed of 1000 rpm. The armature resistance is 0.5 Ω and the field resistance is 110 Ω. Calculate: (a) Back EMF, (b) Total input power, (c) Mechanical power developed, (d) Shaft torque if rotational losses are 500 W.

### Given Data:
- $V = 220$ V, $I_a = 30$ A, $N = 1000$ rpm
- $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$
- $P_{rotational} = 500$ W

### Part (a): Back EMF

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\ \text{A}$$

$$I_L = I_a + I_f = 30 + 2 = 32\ \text{A}$$

Apply KVL to armature:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

$$E_b = 220 - (30)(0.5) = 220 - 15$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 205\ \text{V}}$$

### Part (b): Total Input Power

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 220 \times 32$$

$$\boxed{P_{in} = 7040\ \text{W} = 7.04\ \text{kW}}$$

### Part (c): Mechanical Power Developed (Gross $P_m$)

The gross mechanical power developed equals the power transferred across the air gap (electromagnetic conversion):

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 205 \times 30$$

$$\boxed{P_m = 6150\ \text{W} = 6.15\ \text{kW}}$$

**Verification using power-flow approach:**

$$P_{in} - P_{cu(a)} - P_{cu(f)} = P_m$$

$$7040 - (30^2 \times 0.5) - (220 \times 2) = 7040 - 450 - 440 = 6150\ \text{W} \ \checkmark$$

### Part (d): Shaft Torque

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{rotational} = 6150 - 500 = 5650\ \text{W}$$

$$T_{shaft} = \frac{P_{out} \times 60}{2 \pi N} = \frac{5650 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1000}$$

$$\boxed{T_{shaft} = 53.95\ \text{N·m}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (3 Motor Types)

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important comparison for KTU short-answer questions.**

| Property | DC Shunt | DC Series | DC Compound (Cumulative) |
|----------|----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| $I_f$ | $V/R_{sh}$ (constant) | $= I_a$ (varies) | Combined effect |
| $\phi$ | Approximately constant | $\propto I_a$ | Mostly constant + boost with load |
| $N$ vs $I_a$ | Slight linear drop | Sharp hyperbolic drop | Moderate drop |
| $T$ vs $I_a$ | Linear (T ∝ I_a) | Parabolic (T ∝ I_a²) | Nearly parabolic |
| Starting Torque | Low (1.2–1.5 × FL) | Very high (2.5–3 × FL) | High (2–2.5 × FL) |
| Speed Regulation | Good (5–15%) | Poor | Fair to good |
| No-Load Operation | Safe (no runaway) | **DANGEROUS (runaway)** | Safe |
| Typical Load | Fans, lathes, pumps | Traction, hoists, cranes | Presses, elevators, shears |
| KTU Mnemonic | "**S**teady & **S**afe" | "**S**trong **S**tarter" | "**S**ensible blend" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram — DC Shunt Motor with Rope-Brake Dynamometer

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the experimental test setup used in PCPEL508 labs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCSupply[DC Supply 220V] --> Ammeter1[Ammeter I_L]
    Ammeter1 --> ShuntField[Shunt Field Winding R_sh]
    Ammeter1 --> ArmatureTerm[A]
    ArmatureTerm --> Ammeter2[Ammeter I_a]
    Ammeter2 --> Armature[Armature R_a]
    ArmatureTerm --> VoltmeterV[V: 220V DC]
    Armature --> BrakeDrum[Brake Drum]
    BrakeDrum --> RopeBrake[Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    RopeBrake --> Spring1[Spring Balance F1]
    RopeBrake --> Spring2[Spring Balance F2]
    BrakeDrum --> TachoMeter[Tachometer N rpm]
    BrakeDrum --> LoadKnob[Load Tightening Knob]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Power Flow in a DC Motor (Stage-by-Stage Energy Conversion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputP[Input Power V x I_L] --> CuLossA[Armature Cu Loss I_a^2 x R_a]
    InputP --> CuLossF[Field Cu Loss V x I_f]
    CuLossA --> AirGap[Power at Air Gap E_b x I_a]
    CuLossF --> AirGap
    AirGap --> MechLoss[Mechanical Loss Friction + Windage]
    AirGap --> IronLoss[Iron Core Loss Hysteresis + Eddy]
    MechLoss --> ShaftOut[Shaft Output Power]
    IronLoss --> ShaftOut
    ShaftOut --> FinalOut[Final Useful Output 2 pi N T / 60]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Procedure for Conducting the Characteristic Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Inspect Motor Nameplate]) --> Step1[Connect Circuit as per Diagram]
    Step1 --> Step2[Apply Rated Voltage without Load]
    Step2 --> Step3[Record No-Load N_0 and I_a0]
    Step3 --> Step4[Tighten Brake Drum Gradually]
    Step4 --> Step5[Record F1 F2 and N for each Load Step]
    Step5 --> Step6{More Load Steps?}
    Step6 -->|Yes| Step4
    Step6 -->|No| Step7[Compute T P_in P_out Efficiency]
    Step7 --> Step8[Plot N vs I_a and T vs I_a curves]
    Step8 --> End([End: Analyze Characteristics])
```

## 4.4 Expected Characteristic Curves — Visual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ShuntChar[Shunt Motor Curves]
        ShuntA[Speed vs I_a: Slight Linear Drop]
        ShuntB[Torque vs I_a: Linear Rise]
    end
    subgraph SeriesChar[Series Motor Curves]
        SeriesA[Speed vs I_a: Sharp Hyperbolic Drop]
        SeriesB[Torque vs I_a: Parabolic Rise]
    end
    subgraph CompoundChar[Compound Motor Curves]
        CompoundA[Speed vs I_a: Moderate Drop]
        CompoundB[Torque vs I_a: Parabolic + Offset]
    end
```

## 4.5 Equivalent Circuit — DC Shunt Motor (Module-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NodeV[Positive Terminal V] --> RshPath[Field: R_sh in parallel]
    NodeV --> RaPath[Armature: R_a in series with Back EMF E_b]
    RshPath --> NodeG[Negative Terminal]
    RaPath --> NodeG
    RaPath -.generates.-> MechOut[Mech Output: T and N]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the speed characteristics of a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor. Why is a DC series motor never started on no-load?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Field Connection | Parallel to armature | Series with armature |
| Field Current ($I_f$) | Constant ($V/R_{sh}$) | Variable ($= I_a$) |
| Flux ($\phi$) | Approximately constant | $\propto I_a$ (before saturation) |
| Speed Equation | $N \propto 1/\phi$ → constant | $N \propto 1/I_a$ → varies widely |
| Speed Regulation | Good (nearly constant) | Poor |
| No-Load Operation | Safe | Dangerous (runaway) |

**Why no-load starting of a series motor is dangerous:**
On no-load, $I_a$ is very small, so $\phi$ becomes very small. From $N = V / (k\phi)$, as $\phi \to 0$, the speed $N \to \infty$ — leading to **mechanical destruction of the armature** due to centrifugal forces. Hence, series motors are always **mechanically coupled to the load** (e.g., through a gearbox) before starting.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Define "speed regulation" of a DC motor. What is its typical value for a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *Speed regulation* is the change in speed of a motor from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of the full-load speed.

$$S_R = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\ \text{\%}$$

**Typical Values:**

| Motor Type | Speed Regulation |
|------------|-----------------|
| DC Shunt Motor | 5% to 15% (good regulation) |
| DC Series Motor | Very high (poor regulation; speed varies widely with load) |
| Cumulative Compound Motor | 15% to 30% (fair regulation) |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct the load test (brake test) on a DC shunt motor. Tabulate the observations and the calculations to be performed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 Ω and a field resistance of 125 Ω. At no-load, the motor takes 5 A and runs at 1200 rpm. At full load, it takes 40 A. Find: (i) Speed at full load, (ii) Torque developed at full load, (iii) Shaft torque if rotational losses = 600 W.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Apparatus Required:** DC shunt motor, rope-brake dynamometer, two spring balances, tachometer, voltmeter, ammeters (line and field), connecting wires.

**Circuit Diagram Description:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above for Mermaid schematic)
- Armature is connected in series with an ammeter, voltmeter, and starter.
- Field winding is connected across the supply in parallel.
- Rope brake passes over a water-cooled pulley fixed to the motor shaft.

**Procedure:**
1. Connect the circuit as per the diagram and insert the starters in the OFF position.
2. Keep the rope brake loose (no-load condition).
3. Start the motor using the 3-point starter; gradually increase voltage to rated value.
4. Apply rated field current using the field rheostat.
5. Note no-load readings: $V, I_{L0}, N_0$ and ambient temperature.
6. Gradually tighten the brake drum to apply load in steps of ~10%.
7. For each load, record $F_1$ (tangential), $F_2$ (dead weight side), $N$ (rpm), $I_L$ (line current).
8. Continue till full load or rated current is reached.
9. Loosen brake, switch off supply.

**Tabulation:**

| $S.No.$ | $I_L$ (A) | $I_f$ (A) | $I_a = I_L - I_f$ (A) | $F_1$ (kgf) | $F_2$ (kgf) | Net Force $(F_1-F_2)$ | $N$ (rpm) | $R$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Calculations (per observation):**
- $T_{shaft} = (F_1 - F_2) \times R \times 9.81$ N·m
- $P_{in} = V \times I_L$ W
- $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{shaft}}{60}$ W
- $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\ \%$

**Plots Required:** (i) N vs I_a, (ii) T vs I_a, (iii) η vs P_out, (iv) N vs P_out

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 125\ \Omega$, $I_{L0} = 5$ A, $N_0 = 1200$ rpm, $I_{FL} = 40$ A.

**Step 1 — Calculate field current (assumed constant):**

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — No-load armature current and back EMF:**

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_f = 5 - 2 = 3\ \text{A}$$

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 250 - (3)(0.3) = 250 - 0.9 = 249.1\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Full-load armature current:**

$$I_{a,FL} = I_{FL} - I_f = 40 - 2 = 38\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Full-load back EMF:**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (38)(0.3) = 250 - 11.4 = 238.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Speed at full load (assuming $\phi$ constant):**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_0} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b0}}$$

$$N_{FL} = N_0 \times \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b0}} = 1200 \times \frac{238.6}{249.1}$$

$$\boxed{N_{FL} = 1149.4\ \text{rpm}}$$

**Step 6 — Torque developed at full load (electromagnetic torque $T_a$):**

$$T_a = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\frac{2\pi N}{60}} = \frac{60 \cdot E_b \cdot I_a}{2\pi N}$$

$$T_a = \frac{60 \times 238.6 \times 38}{2\pi \times 1149.4}$$

$$T_a = \frac{543996}{7222.2} = 75.32\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_a = 75.32\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Step 7 — Shaft torque (subtracting rotational losses):**

$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 238.6 \times 38 = 9066.8\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{out} = P_{mech} - P_{rotational} = 9066.8 - 600 = 8466.8\ \text{W}$$

$$T_{shaft} = \frac{60 \times P_{out}}{2\pi N_{FL}} = \frac{60 \times 8466.8}{2\pi \times 1149.4}$$

$$\boxed{T_{shaft} = 70.34\ \text{N·m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the no-load and load characteristics of a DC compound motor (cumulative type). Compare its performance with DC shunt and DC series motors.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 200 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of 0.2 Ω and a series field resistance of 0.05 Ω. The motor draws 50 A from the supply and runs at 800 rpm. Find: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Power developed, (iii) Torque developed, (iv) Speed when the load is increased such that the motor draws 80 A (assume unsaturated field, so $\phi \propto I_a$).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Characteristics of Cumulative Compound Motor:**

The cumulative compound motor has **both** shunt and series field windings wound such that their fluxes **add (aid each other)**.

**(1) No-Load Characteristic (Speed vs Field Current $I_f$):**
- At no-load, the series field flux is negligible (since $I_a$ is small).
- Motor behaves almost like a shunt motor.
- $\phi \approx \phi_{sh}$ (constant) → Speed is nearly constant.

**(2) Load Characteristic (Speed vs Armature Current $I_a$):**
- As $I_a$ increases, series field adds to shunt field → $\phi$ increases with load.
- $N = (V - I_a R_a) / (k \phi)$ → Both numerator decreases and denominator increases → **speed drops more rapidly** than in a shunt motor.

**(3) Torque vs Armature Current:**
- $T \propto \phi I_a$, where $\phi = \phi_{sh} + \phi_{se} \propto (I_{sh} + k I_a)$.
- Below saturation, the curve lies **above the shunt motor line** and **below the pure series parabola**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Shunt | Series | Cumulative Compound |
|----------|-------|--------|---------------------|
| Speed Regulation | Good | Poor | Fair (between the two) |
| Starting Torque | Low | Very High | High |
| No-Load Safety | Safe | **Unsafe** | Safe (shunt field limits $\phi_{min}$) |
| Speed Drop with Load | Slight | Steep | Moderate |

**Application:** Punch presses, elevators, rolling mills, shears.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V = 200$ V, $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05\ \Omega$, $I_1 = 50$ A, $N_1 = 800$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Back EMF at 50 A:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_1(R_a + R_{se}) = 200 - 50(0.2 + 0.05)$$

$$E_{b1} = 200 - 50(0.25) = 200 - 12.5$$

$$\boxed{E_{b1} = 187.5\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 2 — Power developed (electromagnetic):**

$$P_{d1} = E_{b1} \cdot I_1 = 187.5 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{P_{d1} = 9375\ \text{W} = 9.375\ \text{kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Torque developed at 50 A:**

$$T_1 = \frac{60 \cdot P_{d1}}{2\pi N_1} = \frac{60 \times 9375}{2\pi \times 800}$$

$$T_1 = \frac{562500}{5026.5}$$

$$\boxed{T_1 = 111.92\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Speed at 80 A (unsaturated field: $\phi_2/\phi_1 = I_2/I_1 = 80/50 = 1.6$):**

$$E_{b2} = V - I_2(R_a + R_{se}) = 200 - 80(0.25) = 200 - 20 = 180\ \text{V}$$

Using $N \propto E_b / \phi$:

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2} = \frac{180}{187.5} \times \frac{50}{80}$$

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = 0.96 \times 0.625 = 0.6$$

$$N_2 = 0.6 \times 800$$

$$\boxed{N_2 = 480\ \text{rpm}}$$

**Verification / Insight:** As load on a series motor increases, the speed drops drastically (from 800 to 480 rpm) — confirming the **inverse speed-torque characteristic** typical of traction applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_a$:** In a **shunt motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_f$, NOT $I_a = I_L$. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kgf to N:** Torque $T = (F_1 - F_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81$. Using 9.81 is mandatory for SI units. Mark deduction: **0.5 to 1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Using $P = V I_L$ for output:** Output is **mechanical** power from the shaft, NOT electrical input. Mark deduction: **2 marks** (concept error).
>
> 4. **Neglecting rotational losses:** When asked for "shaft torque," subtract rotational losses from gross mechanical power. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract the field copper loss** in efficiency calculation: $P_{in} = V \times I_L$ — but to find $P_m$, you must subtract both $I_a^2 R_a$ AND $V I_f$.
>
> 6. **Plotting $N$ vs $T$ instead of $N$ vs $I_a$:** The standard "characteristic test" curves are:
> - (i) Speed vs Armature Current
> - (ii) Torque vs Armature Current
> - (iii) Efficiency vs Output Power
> - (iv) Speed vs Output Power
>
> 7. **Units inconsistency:** $N$ in **rpm** (NOT rad/s) when using $P = 2\pi N T / 60$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DC Motor Characteristics (PCPEL508 Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **The 3 Governing Equations:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$; $E_b = k \phi N$; $T_a = k \phi I_a$
>
> ✅ **The 3 Motor Types:** Shunt (constant speed), Series (high starting torque, variable speed), Compound (balanced)
>
> ✅ **The 3 Key Curves to Plot:** N vs I_a, T vs I_a, η vs P_out (plus optional: N vs P_out)
>
> ✅ **The 3 Power Quantities:** $P_{in} = V I_L$, $P_{developed} = E_b I_a$, $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N T / 60$
>
> ✅ **The 3 Losses to Track:** Armature Cu loss ($I_a^2 R_a$), Field Cu loss ($V I_f$ for shunt), Rotational losses (friction + windage + core)
>
> ✅ **Critical Safety Rule:** NEVER start a DC series motor on no-load → **runaway condition**.
>
> ✅ **Speed Regulation Formula:** $S_R = (N_{NL} - N_{FL}) / N_{FL} \times 100\%$ — **smaller is better**.
>
> ✅ **Torque Conversion:** $T = (F_1 - F_2) R \cdot 9.81$ (in N·m) — always use **9.81 m/s²**.
>
> ✅ **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100$ — always expressed in **%**.
>
> ✅ **Series Motor Speed Law:** $N \propto 1/I_a$ (unsaturated) — **inverse characteristic**.
>
> ✅ **Shunt Motor Speed:** Nearly constant, drops by 5–15% from no-load to full-load.
>
> ✅ **Cumulative Compound:** Series flux **aids** shunt flux → higher starting torque.
>
> ✅ **Differential Compound:** Series flux **opposes** shunt flux → rarely used (negative speed regulation).
>
> ✅ **Experimental Tip:** For the lab record, always tabulate observations in **6 columns** minimum: $I_L$, $I_f$, $I_a$, $F_1$, $F_2$, $N$.
>
> ✅ **Graphs to be drawn on graph sheet:** Use **4-quadrant** layout — N vs I_a (top-left), T vs I_a (top-right), η vs P_out (bottom-left), N vs P_out (bottom-right).
>
> ✅ **Real-world link:** DC shunt motors are the **prime movers for centrifugal pumps** in the next lab experiment (PCPEL508 Module 3) — so mastering the N-T curves is essential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Tests on DC Generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Tests on DC Generators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Generator** is a rotating electrical machine that converts mechanical energy into direct current electrical energy based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The **Characteristic Tests on DC Generators** constitute the standardized laboratory procedures used to determine the steady-state performance parameters of the machine by varying one independent variable (field current, load current, or speed) while keeping the others constant, and observing the resulting dependent electrical quantity (generated EMF or terminal voltage).

The three fundamental characteristics tested are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The Three Cardinal Characteristics**
> 1. **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — also called the **Magnetization Curve** or **No-Load Saturation Curve**.
> 2. **External Characteristic** — also called the **Load Characteristic** or **Performance Curve**.
> 3. **Internal Characteristic** — the relationship between generated EMF ($E_g$) and armature current ($I_a$).

These characteristics describe how the generated EMF ($E_g$) and terminal voltage ($V_t$) vary with field current ($I_f$), armature current ($I_a$), and load current ($I_L$) under specified operating conditions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle fitted with a dynamo (generator)** that powers a small LED bulb:

- The faster you pedal → the **brighter** the bulb → more **EMF** generated.
- Now suppose you **reduce the strength** of the magnet inside the dynamo (this is exactly what **field current** does in a DC generator — it controls the magnetic field strength).
- The bulb dims even at the same pedaling speed because the **magnetic flux is weaker**.

> The relationship between *how strong the magnetic field is* and *how much voltage is produced* at a given speed is what the **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** captures. It is the "personality fingerprint" of every DC machine.

Now extend the analogy:

- When you connect a **heavy load** to the dynamo (say, a larger headlamp), the voltage at the bulb terminals **drops** because of internal losses. This voltage drop versus load is the **External Characteristic**.
- And the voltage drop *inside* the generator itself (before terminal losses) is the **Internal Characteristic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: Think of the OCC as the machine's "DNA curve." It tells you, for a given field current, what EMF the machine *wants* to generate at rated speed. Any deviation from this ideal EMF in actual loaded operation is due to **armature reaction**, **armature resistance drop ($I_a R_a$)**, and **brush contact drop**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Units & Sign Conventions

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Standard Notation in KTU |
|----------|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| Generated EMF | $E_g$ | Volt (V) | $E_g$ or $E$ |
| Terminal Voltage | $V_t$ | Volt (V) | $V$ or $V_t$ |
| Armature Current | $I_a$ | Ampere (A) | $I_a$ |
| Field Current | $I_f$ | Ampere (A) | $I_f$ |
| Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh}$ | Ampere (A) | $I_{sh}$ |
| Load Current | $I_L$ | Ampere (A) | $I_L$ |
| Magnetic Flux per pole | $\Phi$ | Weber (Wb) | $\Phi$ |
| Speed of rotation | $N$ | RPM | $N$ |
| Angular Speed | $\omega$ | rad/s | $\omega$ |
| Armature Resistance | $R_a$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | $R_a$ |
| Shunt Field Resistance | $R_{sh}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | $R_{sh}$ |
| Series Field Resistance | $R_{se}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | $R_{se}$ |
| Series Field Turns | $N_{se}$ | Turns | $N_{se}$ |
| Shunt Field Turns | $N_{sh}$ | Turns | $N_{sh}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention**: The standard machine constant $K_a$ combines all machine constructional parameters. The fundamental EMF equation is $E_g = K_a \, \Phi \, \omega$. In KTU exam scripts, students must explicitly mention that the machine is operated at **rated speed (constant N)** while plotting OCC.

---

## 1.4 Classification of DC Generators Relevant to Characteristic Testing

| Type | Field Connection | Characteristic Behaviour |
|------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Separately Excited** | Field winding fed from external DC source | Independent control of $I_f$ and $I_a$ |
| **Self-Excited Shunt** | Field winding in **parallel** with armature | Field current ≈ (1–5)% of $I_a$ |
| **Self-Excited Series** | Field winding in **series** with armature | Field current = $I_a$ |
| **Self-Excited Compound** (Cumulative) | Both shunt and series fields aiding | Improved voltage regulation |
| **Self-Excited Compound** (Differential) | Series field opposes shunt field | Constant current characteristic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Governing EMF Equation

The operation of every DC generator — irrespective of whether it is separately excited, shunt, series, or compound — is fundamentally governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The instantaneous EMF induced in a single armature conductor is $e = B \, l \, v$, where $B$ is the flux density, $l$ is the active length of the conductor, and $v$ is the relative velocity.

When this microscopic EMF is summed over all conductors arranged in the armature winding, the macroscopic expression becomes:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

Where:
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Webers)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature in **RPM**
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding

Since $\dfrac{Z \, P}{60 \, A}$ is a constant for a given machine, we group it into a single constant $K_a$:

$$E_g = K_a \, \Phi \, N$$

Or in terms of angular velocity ($\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$):

$$E_g = K'_a \, \Phi \, \omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for characteristic tests**: This single equation tells us that $E_g$ depends on **only two variables** — flux $\Phi$ and speed $N$. By fixing $N$ (rated speed) and varying $\Phi$ (via $I_f$), we plot the **OCC**. By fixing $I_f$ (and hence $\Phi$) and varying $I_L$, we plot the **External and Internal Characteristics**.

---

## 2.2 The Three Characteristics — Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

### A. Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC) — $E_g$ vs $I_f$ at $N = N_{rated}$, $I_L = 0$

**Operational Logic:**
1. The machine is driven at **constant rated speed** by a prime mover (usually a calibrated DC motor or 3-phase induction motor with VFD).
2. The field winding is fed from a **separate variable DC source** (for a separately excited test) or a **rheostat-controlled self-excitation**.
3. Field current $I_f$ is **gradually increased from zero** to a value that produces approximately **125% of rated voltage** (so the knee of the curve is captured).
4. At each step, the **open-circuit terminal voltage** (= generated EMF, since $I_a = 0$) is measured using a **high-resistance voltmeter** to avoid loading the armature.
5. $I_f$ is then **decreased in steps** to zero. The descending and ascending curves are plotted — any gap between them indicates **hysteresis** in the iron magnetic circuit.

**Shape of the Curve:**
- **Initial linear (straight line) region**: The iron is unsaturated, so $\Phi \propto I_f$ (linear B-H curve).
- **Knee (bend) region**: The iron begins to saturate; further increase in $I_f$ produces progressively smaller increase in $\Phi$ and hence $E_g$.
- **Saturation region**: The iron is heavily saturated; $\Phi$ increases very slowly with $I_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the OCC is non-linear**: The relation between flux $\Phi$ and field MMF ($N_f I_f$) is governed by the **B-H curve of the armature iron**. Initially in the unsaturated region, $\Phi = \dfrac{N_f I_f}{\mathcal{R}}$ (reluctance $\mathcal{R}$ is constant). As saturation sets in, reluctance effectively increases, and the curve bends over.

### B. External Characteristic — $V_t$ vs $I_L$ at $N = N_{rated}$, $I_f = I_{f,rated}$

**Operational Logic:**
1. The machine is self-excited (or separately excited) at the rated field current.
2. Load is applied in steps from **no-load to full-load to overload** (usually up to 125% of rated current).
3. At each load, the **terminal voltage $V_t$** and **load current $I_L$** are recorded.
4. The field rheostat is **NOT adjusted** during the test (i.e., field circuit resistance is held constant).

**Why $V_t$ drops with $I_L$:**
- Armature reaction **demagnetizing effect** reduces net flux $\Phi$ → reduces $E_g$.
- Ohmic drop $I_a R_a$ inside the armature → reduces terminal voltage.

For a **shunt generator**: $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$, with $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ and $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$.

### C. Internal Characteristic — $E_g$ vs $I_a$ at $N = N_{rated}$, $I_f = I_{f,rated}$

This curve is **derived** (not directly measured). For each load point:
- $I_a$ is computed: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ (for shunt).
- $E_g$ is computed: $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a + \text{brush contact drop}$.

The internal characteristic lies **above** the external characteristic by exactly the armature resistance drop and brush drop.

---

## 2.3 Critical Resistance & Critical Speed — Concepts Used in OCC Analysis

### Critical Field Resistance (R_c)

**Definition**: The value of total field circuit resistance at which the generator just **fails to build up voltage** when the shunt field is connected across the armature.

**Geometric interpretation on OCC**: The **critical resistance line** is drawn from the origin as a **straight line tangent to the initial linear portion of the OCC**. Its slope = $\dfrac{V}{I_f}$ at the tangent point.

- If **actual field resistance > R_c** → machine **will not self-excite**.
- If **actual field resistance < R_c** → machine **will self-excite** and build up to the intersection of the field resistance line with the OCC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked**: "Why is the tangent drawn to the *initial linear portion* and not to the saturation region?" Because in the linear region, residual flux is being amplified proportionally. Once saturation begins, additional resistance has little effect — the tangent interpretation is rooted in linear stability theory.

### Critical Speed (N_c)

**Definition**: The speed below which a shunt generator will **not build up voltage** for a given field circuit resistance.

$$N_c = N_{rated} \times \dfrac{\text{Slope of field resistance line}}{\text{Slope of OCC at corresponding point}}$$

Equivalently, the OCC for any speed $N_2$ is obtained by **vertically scaling** the OCC at rated speed $N_1$ by the factor $N_2 / N_1$.

---

## 2.4 Voltage Regulation — The Key Performance Metric

**Definition**: The percentage change in terminal voltage when the load is removed from full-load to no-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage, with field excitation and speed held constant.

$$\text{Voltage Regulation (VR)} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 \, \%$$

For different DC generator types (at constant speed and field rheostat setting):

| Generator Type | Approximate VR (Typical) | Reason |
|----------------|-------------------------|--------|
| Separately Excited | 5–10% | Only armature reaction + $I_a R_a$ drop |
| Shunt | 8–15% | Above + reduced $I_{sh}$ at lower $V_t$ |
| Series | Poor (rising characteristic) | $I_L = I_f$, so $\Phi$ varies drastically |
| Cumulative Compound | 3–8% (best) | Series field compensates drop |
| Differential Compound | Negative (rising $V$ with load) | Series field opposes & weakens net flux |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they account for >85% of KTU ESE marks on DC Generator Characteristics.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_g = K_a \, \Phi \, N$ | Fundamental EMF equation | $K_a = \dfrac{Z P}{60 A}$ |
| 2 | $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a - \text{brush drop}$ | Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (Generator) | Brush drop ≈ 2 V total |
| 3 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ | Expanded EMF equation | All four quantities are machine/SI |
| 4 | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | KCL at armature node (shunt gen.) | For series: $I_a = I_L$ |
| 5 | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$ | Shunt field current | Independent of $I_a$ |
| 6 | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage Regulation | Expressed as percentage |
| 7 | $\text{Efficiency} \eta = \dfrac{V_t \cdot I_L}{V_t \cdot I_L + \text{Losses}} \times 100$ | Commercial efficiency | Losses = Cu + Iron + Mech |
| 8 | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_f} \vert_{\text{tangent point}}$ | Critical field resistance | Geometric from OCC |
| 9 | $N_c = N_{rated} \cdot \dfrac{\text{slope of field line}}{\text{slope of OCC}}$ | Critical speed | For given field resistance |
| 10 | $\dfrac{E_{g2}}{E_{g1}} = \dfrac{N_2 \, \Phi_2}{N_1 \, \Phi_1}$ | Scaling law for EMF | Used for variable-speed OCC |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these characteristic tests matter in industry:**

- **Generator Manufacturing**: Every DC generator manufactured (by BHEL, Siemens, ABB, Crompton Greaves) is run through these characteristic tests on a test bench before dispatch. The OCC validates the design flux and confirms magnetic circuit integrity.
- **Power Plants**: In standby DC generator sets (used for emergency power in substations, hospitals, data centres), periodic OCC verification confirms residual magnetism — critical for reliable self-excitation.
- **Automotive**: The "load characteristic" of automotive alternators (when rectified) is used by ECU designers to predict battery charging behaviour at varying engine RPM.
- **Marine Propulsion**: DC generators on ships must maintain voltage regulation within ±2.5% — cumulative compounding is used, and the compounding curve (no-load to full-load) is a contractual test.
- **Servo Systems & Robotics**: Separately excited DC generators are used as **DC tachometers** (sensors) — the linear portion of OCC is the operating region.
