Then: - Step frequency: $f_{step} = 1 / T_{delay}$ - Time for one revolution: $T_{rev} = N \cdot T_{delay}$ - Revolutions per second: $f_{rev} = 1 / T_{rev} = 1 / (N \cdot T_{delay})$ - Revolutions per minute: $$N_{rpm} = \frac{60}{N \cdot T_{delay}}$$ **Numerical Solution (4 marks):** Given: $N_{full} = 200$ steps/rev, $T_{delay} = 4$ ms = 0.004 s, half-step mode. **(i) Effective step angle (1 mark):** $$\theta_{eff} = \frac{\theta_s}{2} = \frac{1.8°}{2} = 0.9°$$ **(ii) Steps per second (1 mark):** $$f_{step} = \frac{1}{T_{delay}} = \frac{1}{0.004} = 250 \text{ steps/sec}$$ **(iii) Shaft rpm (2 marks):** In half-step mode, total steps per revolution = $2N = 400$. $$N_{rpm} = \frac{60}{400 \cdot 0.004} = \frac{60}{1.6} = 37.5 \text{ rpm}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Hot-Spots:** > 1. **Confusing step angle with step frequency:** Step angle is a *geometric* property of the motor (degrees), not the same as the *temporal* step rate (Hz). Mixing units is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU lab viva. > 2. **Forgetting to use 2N in half-step rpm formula:** Many students use 200 instead of 400 in the rpm calculation for half-step mode. This is a **2-mark deduction** guaranteed in the official scheme. > 3. **Not mentioning the COM-pin flyback diode:** A complete block diagram in Q-A(a) *must* show the +V supply tied to ULN2003 COM. Skipping this costs **1 mark**. > 4. **Driving coils directly from 8051:** Stating "8051 can drive the motor directly" is a fundamental circuit-design error. Deduct **2 marks**. > 5. **Using `while(1)` in a finite loop or vice versa:** For continuous rotation, an *infinite outer loop* is mandatory. A finite `for(i=0;i<200;i++)` produces exactly 1 revolution and stops — **2-mark deduction** in programming questions. > 6. **Incorrect `delay_ms` constant:** Using `j<1275` blindly without deriving it from the 11.0592 MHz clock is acceptable for Keil-C lab work, but in *board* answer scripts, a one-line justification is expected for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - A **stepper motor** rotates by a **fixed step angle (θₛ = 1.8°)** per digital pulse — no feedback encoder required (open-loop). - The **8051 port pins** can source/sink only **≈ 1.6 mA**; the **ULN2003** boosts this to **500 mA** per channel. - The **four phase coils** are connected to **P1.0–P1.3** through the ULN2003 inputs/outputs. - The **ULN2003 COM pin** must be tied to the **motor's positive supply** for the internal flyback diodes to clamp the inductive kick. - **Wave drive** = low torque, low power; **Full-step** = highest torque, double power; **Half-step** = best resolution, torque ripple. - The **lookup table** stored in `code` memory is the heart of the software — direction reversal = reverse the table index order. - **Delay routine** is generated by a *nested for-loop*; the inner count is calibrated from the 11.0592 MHz crystal's machine cycle of 1.085 µs. - **Speed formula:** $N_{rpm} = 60 / (N \cdot T_{delay})$ — for half-step, replace $N$ with $2N$. - **Standard lab motor:** 200 steps/rev, 4-phase unipolar, θₛ = 1.8°. - **8051 registers** used: `P1` (output port), `P3` (input switches), `DPTR` + `MOVC` for lookup table access (assembly), or simply an array in C. - **One electrical cycle** = 4 full steps in wave/full-step mode, but **8 half-steps** in half-step mode. - **Stall condition** occurs if $T_{delay}$ is too small — rotor cannot reach detent before the next pulse arrives, and steps are *lost without any error indication* (open-loop caveat). - The **freewheeling diode** in ULN2003 prevents reverse-polarity damage; an *external* 1N4007 across each coil is *not* required when ULN2003 is used. - **Viva Favourite:** "Why use an 11.0592 MHz crystal and not 12 MHz?" — Answer: 11.0592 MHz generates an exact **9600 baud** rate for serial communication (1.085 µs × 3 = 3.255 µs ≈ 1/307200, evenly divisible), making it the universal embedded-systems frequency.

topics.title: Assembly Language Programs for Simple Tasks Using 8085 Microprocessor Kit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language Programs for Simple Tasks Using 8085 Microprocessor Kit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: ASSEMBLY LANGUAGE PROGRAMS FOR SIMPLE TASKS USING 8085 MICROPROCESSOR KIT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intel 8085 Microprocessor** is an **8-bit** parallel register-oriented Central Processing Unit (CPU) fabricated on a single LSI (Large Scale Integration) chip using NMOS technology. It operates on a single **+5V** power supply with a clock frequency of **3.072 MHz** (internal) derived from an external **6.144 MHz** crystal. The processor features a **16-bit Programmable Address Bus** (capable of addressing **64 KB** of memory) and a bidirectional **8-bit Data Bus**.

**Assembly Language** for the 8085 is a low-level symbolic programming language that uses **mnemonics** (English-like abbreviations) to represent the binary machine codes (opcodes) executed directly by the processor. Each instruction is a 1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte instruction containing the opcode, operand(s), and data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> As per the PCPEL508 lab module, students must write, assemble, and execute assembly language programs for simple arithmetic, logical, data transfer, and code conversion tasks on the 8085 microprocessor training kit, observe register/memory contents, and verify results.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **8085 microprocessor** as a highly disciplined factory supervisor:

- The **Accumulator (Register A)** is the supervisor's personal workbench — every arithmetic or logical operation is performed here.
- The **Registers (B, C, D, E, H, L)** are six small in-desk drawers where the supervisor temporarily stores materials (data).
- The **Program Counter (PC)** is a clipboard that points to the next instruction to be read.
- The **Instruction Decoder** is the supervisor's brain that reads each instruction and directs workers (ALU) what to do.
- **Memory (RAM/ROM)** is the factory's storeroom containing both instructions and raw data.
- The **Data Bus** is a conveyor belt (8 lanes wide) and the **Address Bus** is the address label on the storeroom shelves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Metrics (Bold Constants):**
> - Data Bus Width = **8 bits**
> - Address Bus Width = **16 bits**
> - Memory Addressable = **2^16 = 65,536 bytes = 64 KB**
> - General Purpose Registers = **7 × 8-bit** (A, B, C, D, E, H, L)
> - Register Pairs = **3 × 16-bit** (BC, DE, HL)
> - Stack Pointer (SP) = **16-bit**
> - Program Counter (PC) = **16-bit**
> - Status Flags = **5** (S, Z, AC, P, CY)
> - Interrupt Pins = **5** (TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, INTR)

### 1.3 8085 Microprocessor Kit — Overview

The 8085 Microprocessor Training Kit is a self-contained educational system comprising:

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **CPU Section** | Houses the 8085 chip, clock generator, and reset circuit |
| **Memory Section** | EPROM (Monitor Program) + RAM (User Program Area) |
| **I/O Section** | Hex Keypad (Input), 7-Segment Displays (Output) |
| **Address/Data Display** | LEDs to monitor bus activity in real time |
| **Power Supply** | +5V, +12V, -12V, -5V regulated DC supply |

> [!TIP]
> Most KTU-lab kits allocate user RAM from memory address **2000H** to **FFFFH** (Monitor occupies 0000H–1FFFH). Always place your program starting at **2000H** to avoid monitor conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 8085 Programming Model

The 8085 has a clean, well-defined programming model with which the student must be fluent. The diagram below summarizes all programmer-accessible registers.

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  ACCUMULATOR (A)         [8-bit]   FLAGS (F)     |
|  8085 uses 8-bit 'A' as primary ALU operand.      |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  B [8]  | C [8]   ->  BC Pair (16-bit addressable)|
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  D [8]  | E [8]   ->  DE Pair (16-bit addressable)|
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  H [8]  | L [8]   ->  HL Pair (Memory Pointer)    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  Program Counter (PC)   [16-bit]                  |
|  Stack Pointer   (SP)  [16-bit]                  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

## 2.2 Status Flag Register (F) — Bit-by-Bit Anatomy

The 8085 packs five status flags and three unused bits into a single 8-bit register (PSW — Program Status Word).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSW} = \;& \underbrace{D_7}_{S} \; \underbrace{D_6}_{Z} \; \underbrace{D_5}_{0} \; \underbrace{D_4}_{AC} \; \underbrace{D_3}_{0} \; \underbrace{D_2}_{P} \; \underbrace{D_1}_{1} \; \underbrace{D_0}_{CY}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Bit | Flag | Symbol | Set When (1) | Affected By |
|-----|------|--------|--------------|-------------|
| D7 | Sign | **S** | Result MSB = 1 (negative) | Most arithmetic/logical ops |
| D6 | Zero | **Z** | Result = 00H | Most arithmetic/logical ops |
| D4 | Aux Carry | **AC** | Carry from D3 to D4 | ADD, SUB, INR, DCR (binary/BCD) |
| D2 | Parity | **P** | Even number of 1s in result | Most arithmetic/logical ops |
| D0 | Carry | **CY** | Carry out of MSB (ADD) or Borrow (SUB) | ADD, SUB, CMP, rotations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bits D5 and D3 are always 0** and **D1 is always 1** in the PSW. This is a critical concept often asked in KTU exams as: *"What is the value of PSW after `MVI A, FFH` followed by `ADD A`?"* — The answer depends on recognizing these fixed bits.

## 2.3 Instruction Set Categories (High-Yield for KTU)

| Category | Examples | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|----------|----------|-------|----------------|
| **Data Transfer** | MOV, MVI, LXI, LDA, STA, LHLD, SHLD, XCHG | 1–3 | 1–4 |
| **Arithmetic** | ADD, ADI, SUB, SUI, INR, DCR, INX, DCX, DAD | 1–2 | 1–3 |
| **Logical** | ANA, ANI, ORA, ORI, XRA, XRI, CMA, CMP, CPI | 1–2 | 1–2 |
| **Branching** | JMP, JZ, JNZ, JC, JNC, JP, JM, JPE, JPO, CALL, RET | 3 | 3–5 |
| **Stack** | PUSH, POP, PCHL, SPHL, XTHL | 1 | 3–4 |
| **I/O & Machine** | IN, OUT, HLT, NOP, RST, DI, EI, RIM, SIM | 1–2 | 2–4 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Addressing, Memory & Conversions

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **must-know numerical relationships** for the 8085 lab exam. These are not theoretical derivations but are mathematically derivable quantities that students are expected to know cold.

| Quantity | Formula | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Addressable Memory | $2^N$ where $N=$ address bus bits | $2^{16} = 65536$ bytes = 64 KB |
| Number of memory locations | $2^{(\text{Address Bus})}$ | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations |
| Data range (unsigned) | $0$ to $2^n - 1$ | 0 to 255 (8-bit) |
| Data range (signed 2's comp) | $-(2^{n-1})$ to $(2^{n-1} - 1)$ | −128 to +127 (8-bit) |
| Hex to Decimal | $\sum d_i \cdot 16^i$ | $\text{2A}_{16} = 2(16)+10 = 42$ |
| BCD to Decimal | Concatenate upper/lower nibble | $\text{42}_{\text{BCD}} = 42_{10}$ |
| Decimal to BCD | Decompose into tens/units nibbles | $57_{10} \to 0101\,0111 = 57_{\text{BCD}}$ |
| 2's Complement | $\bar{x} + 1$ | $-(+5) = \text{FBH}$ in 8-bit |
| Stack Memory | $\text{SP}$ points to last filled location | PUSH decrements SP first |

## 2.5 Why Assembly Language Programming for 8085?

Even in the era of high-level languages, 8085 ALP study is mandated in the KTU 2024 ECE curriculum for three engineering reasons:

1. **Hardware-Software Co-design Intuition** — Students see exactly how a CPU fetches, decodes, and executes each instruction (the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle).
2. **Embedded Systems Foundation** — Modern microcontrollers (8051, PIC, ARM) retain the 8085's register-load-store philosophy.
3. **Real-time Control** — Mechanical pump control, prime-mover speed governors, and PLCs all rely on time-deterministic code; assembly guarantees cycle-exact execution.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a centrifugal pump control system, an 8085-based controller can read a flow sensor (via IN port), compute the deviation from set-point, and drive a thyristor-firing circuit (via OUT port) within a deterministic 200 µs — impossible to guarantee in C without inline assembly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Programs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm Design Methodology — The 5-Stage Approach

For **every** KTU lab program, follow this rigid structure to score full marks:

| Stage | Description | Deliverable |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **1. Problem Statement** | Restate what is to be computed | One-line English sentence |
| **2. Algorithm** | Step-by-step logic in plain language | Numbered list (3–10 steps) |
| **3. Flowchart** | Visual decision-flow representation | Diamond/rectangle blocks |
| **4. Assembly Code** | 8085 mnemonics with comments | Tabular form with addresses |
| **5. Memory Map** | Where data and program reside | Address-to-content table |

## 3.2 Worked-Out Program 1 — 8-Bit Addition (Two Numbers)

### Problem Statement
Add two 8-bit numbers stored at memory locations **2050H** and **2051H**. Store the result at **2052H** and the carry flag value at **2053H**.

### Algorithm
1. Load the first number from 2050H into the accumulator.
2. Add the second number at 2051H to the accumulator.
3. Store the sum at 2052H.
4. If carry is generated, store 01H at 2053H; else store 00H.
5. Stop execution.

### Memory Map (Before Execution)
| Address | Data | Meaning |
|---------|------|---------|
| 2050H | A5H | First operand |
| 2051H | 3CH | Second operand |
| 2052H | ?? | Result destination |
| 2053H | ?? | Carry destination |

### Assembly Program (Starting at 2000H)
| Address | Label | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment | Machine Code |
|---------|-------|----------|---------|---------|--------------|
| 2000H |      | LXI      | H,2050H | Point HL to first operand | 21 50 20 |
| 2003H |      | MOV      | A,M     | A ← M(2050H) = A5H | 7E |
| 2004H |      | INX      | H       | HL ← 2051H | 23 |
| 2005H |      | ADD      | M       | A ← A + M(2051H) = A5H + 3CH | 86 |
| 2006H |      | INX      | H       | HL ← 2052H | 23 |
| 2007H |      | MOV      | M,A     | Store sum at 2052H | 77 |
| 2008H |      | INX      | H       | HL ← 2053H | 23 |
| 2009H |      | MVI      | A,00H   | A ← 00H (default for no carry) | 3E 00 |
| 200BH |      | JNC      | NEXT    | If CY = 0, skip | D2 12 20 |
| 200EH |      | MVI      | A,01H   | A ← 01H (carry detected) | 3E 01 |
| 2010H | NEXT | STA      | 2053H   | Store carry flag at 2053H | 32 53 20 |
| 2013H |      | HLT      |         | Stop processor | 76 |

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & A \leftarrow M[2050H] = \text{A5}_{16} = 1010\,0101_2 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & A \leftarrow A + M[2051H] = \text{A5}_{16} + \text{3C}_{16} \\
& = 1010\,0101_2 + 0011\,1100_2 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Performing binary addition column-by-column:} \\
& \quad 1\,0100\,0101 \quad \text{(bit 7 generates carry-out = 1)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & A = \text{E1}_{16}, \quad \text{CY flag} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Verification (Decimal Cross-Check)
$$
\begin{aligned}
A5_{16} &= 10 \times 16 + 5 = 165_{10} \\
3C_{16} &= 3 \times 16 + 12 = 60_{10} \\
\text{Sum} &= 165 + 60 = 225_{10} = \text{E1}_{16} \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Carry} &= 1 \quad \text{(since } 225 < 256, \text{ no actual carry in 8-bit)} \\
& \text{Wait — correction: } 165 + 60 = 225 < 256, \text{ so CY = 0}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** A5H + 3CH = E1H with **no carry** (E1H = 225 < 256). Students often incorrectly mark CY = 1 for any addition. CY = 1 only when the sum **exceeds 8 bits** (≥ 100H = 256).

### Final Memory Map (After Execution)
| Address | Data | Meaning |
|---------|------|---------|
| 2050H | A5H | First operand (unchanged) |
| 2051H | 3CH | Second operand (unchanged) |
| 2052H | **E1H** | **Result: 225 decimal** |
| 2053H | **00H** | **No carry generated** |

## 3.3 Worked-Out Program 2 — Find the Largest of Two 8-Bit Numbers

### Problem Statement
Compare the contents of memory locations **2040H** and **2041H**. Store the larger value at **2042H**.

### Assembly Program
| Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| 2000H | LDA | 2040H  | A ← M[2040H] |
| 2003H | MOV | B,A    | B ← first number (backup) |
| 2004H | LDA | 2041H  | A ← M[2041H] |
| 2007H | CMP | B      | Compare A − B (A = second, B = first) |
| 2008H | JNC | STORE  | If CY = 0 (i.e., A ≥ B), jump to STORE |
| 200BH | MOV | A,B    | Else A ← B (first was larger) |
| 200CH | STORE: STA | 2042H | Store larger at 2042H |
| 200FH | HLT |        | Stop |

### Analytical Insight — Why CMP Works
The **CMP B** instruction performs an internal subtraction **A − B** but discards the result, retaining only the flags:

| Condition | CY Flag | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| A ≥ B | 0 | A − B did not borrow |
| A < B | 1 | A − B required borrow |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trivia:** `CMP` is a 1-byte, 1-machine-cycle instruction (opcode **B8** for CMP B). Many students lose marks by using `SUB` and then checking CY — works, but `CMP` is the elegant, preferred method.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Program 3 — Sum of N Numbers (Array Addition)

### Problem Statement
Add **N** 8-bit numbers stored in memory starting at **2051H** (N stored at **2050H**). Store the 16-bit result at **2050H & 2051H** of a separate location **2070H–2071H**.

### Algorithm
1. Load N into register C (counter).
2. Initialize accumulator (A = 0) and carry register (B = 0).
3. Load first number, add to A, propagate carry to B.
4. Increment HL pointer; decrement C; repeat until C = 0.
5. Store A at 2070H, B at 2071H.

### Program
| Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| 2000H | LXI | H,2050H | HL points to count |
| 2003H | MOV | C,M     | C ← N (counter) |
| 2004H | XRA | A       | A ← 0, CY ← 0 |
| 2005H | MVI | B,00H   | B ← 0 (carry register) |
| 2007H | INX | H       | HL points to first data |
| 2008H | BACK: ADD | M   | A ← A + M[HL] |
| 2009H | JNC | SKIP    | If no carry, skip |
| 200CH | INR | B       | B ← B + 1 (propagate carry) |
| 200DH | SKIP: INX | H   | HL ← HL + 1 |
| 200EH | DCR | C       | C ← C − 1 |
| 200FH | JNZ | BACK    | If C ≠ 0, loop |
| 2012H | STA | 2070H   | Store low byte of sum |
| 2015H | MOV | A,B     | A ← carry byte |
| 2016H | STA | 2071H   | Store high byte of sum |
| 2019H | HLT |         | Stop |

### Mathematical Trace
For N = 3, data = {85H, 32H, C7H}:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} \quad & A = 0 + 85\text{H} = 85\text{H}, \; CY = 0, \; B = 0 \\
\text{Iteration 2:} \quad & A = 85\text{H} + 32\text{H} = \text{B7H}, \; CY = 0, \; B = 0 \\
\text{Iteration 3:} \quad & A = \text{B7H} + \text{C7H} = 17\text{H} \;(\text{mod 256}), \; CY = 1, \; B = 1 \\
\text{Final:} \quad & \text{Result} = \text{B + A} = 1\,17\text{H} = 279_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decimal cross-check:** 133 + 50 + 199 = 382 = 17EH ≠ 279? Recompute: 85H = 133, 32H = 50, C7H = 199, sum = 382. But 8-bit sum is 382 mod 256 = 126 = 7EH. With carry, full result = 1 × 256 + 126 = 382. So 16-bit result is **017EH**, not **0117H**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in KTU Valuation:** When students add 0xB7 + 0xC7, they often write 0x17E (three hex digits) as the result instead of carrying properly. The 8-bit accumulator holds 0x7E, and B holds 0x01, making the 16-bit answer **0x017E**.

## 3.5 Worked-Out Program 4 — BCD to 7-Segment Display Code

### Problem Statement
Convert a BCD digit (0–9) stored at **2050H** into its 7-segment code using lookup table logic, store result at **2051H**.

### Lookup Table
| BCD | 7-Segment Code (a b c d e f g dp) | Hex |
|-----|-----------------------------------|-----|
| 0 | 11111100 | FC |
| 1 | 01100000 | 60 |
| 2 | 11011010 | DA |
| 3 | 11110010 | F2 |
| 4 | 01100110 | 66 |
| 5 | 10110110 | B6 |
| 6 | 10111110 | BE |
| 7 | 11100000 | E0 |
| 8 | 11111110 | FE |
| 9 | 11110110 | F6 |

### Program
| Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| 2000H | LXI | H,2050H | HL points to BCD digit |
| 2003H | MOV | A,M     | A ← BCD digit |
| 2004H | CPI | 0AH    | Compare with 10 (validity check) |
| 2006H | JNC | INVALID | If ≥ 10, invalid |
| 2009H | MVI | B,00H   | B ← 0 (index) |
| 200BH | LXI | D,TABLE | DE points to table base |
| 200EH | ADD | B       | A ← A + 0 (offset) |
| 200FH | MOV | L,A     | L ← offset (HL now points to table entry) |
| 2010H | MOV | A,M     | A ← 7-seg code from table |
| 2011H | STA | 2051H   | Store result |
| 2014H | HLT |         | Stop |
| 2015H | INVALID: MVI | A,FFH | A ← FFH (error code) |
| 2017H | STA | 2051H   | |
| 201AH | HLT |         | |
| 201BH | TABLE: DB | FCH,60H,DAH,F2H,66H,B6H,BEH,E0H,FEH,F6H | 7-seg lookup table |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Problem-to-Instruction Mapping

This is the **single most important reference table** for KTU lab viva and exam questions.

| Task | Required Instructions | Key Trick |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| 8-bit Add | MOV, ADD, STA | Use ADD M for memory-direct |
| 8-bit Subtract | SUB, SBB, STA | Use SUI for immediate |
| 16-bit Add | DAD rp, XCHG | DAD adds rp to HL |
| Multiply by 2 | ADD A | Left shift = multiply by 2 |
| Divide by 2 | RRC or STC + RAR | Right shift = divide unsigned by 2 |
| 2's Complement | CMA, INR A or use SUI | Negate = invert + 1 |
| 10's Complement (BCD) | CMA + ADI 01 + DAA | Special BCD trick |
| Mask upper nibble | ANI 0FH | AND with 0000 1111 |
| Mask lower nibble | ANI F0H | AND with 1111 0000 |
| Set upper nibble | ORI F0H | OR with 1111 0000 |
| Toggle bit 5 | XRI 20H | XOR with 0010 0000 |
| Count 1s in byte | Rotate + Carry accumulation | RAR + JNC + INR B |
| Find largest | CMP + JC/JNC branching | CMP sets CY without altering operands |
| Sort array | Nested loop with CMP, JNC, XCHG | Bubble sort pattern |
| BCD to Binary | DAA after ADD | Decimal Adjust Accumulator |
| Hex to ASCII | ORI 30H for 0–9, ORI 37H for A–F | Common conversion |

## 3.7 BCD Subtraction — The 10's Complement Method

For the KTU exam, students must know the **10's complement subtraction** for BCD numbers, used in mechanical counter displays.

### Problem
Subtract BCD number at **2051H** from BCD number at **2050H**, store result at **2052H**.

### 8085 Algorithm
1. Compute 9's complement of subtrahend: `CMA` + `ADI 01H` then `DAA`.
2. Add to minuend: `ADD M` + `DAA`.
3. If no carry, take 10's complement of result and prepend minus sign.
4. If carry, result is positive BCD.

### Program
| Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| 2000H | LXI | H,2050H | HL → minuend |
| 2003H | MOV | A,M     | A ← minuend |
| 2004H | INX | H       | HL → subtrahend |
| 2005H | MOV | B,M     | B ← subtrahend (backup) |
| 2006H | CMA |         | A ← ~subtrahend (1's comp) |
| 2007H | ADI | 01H     | A ← 2's comp (binary negate) |
| 2009H | DAA |         | Now A = 9's comp of BCD subtrahend |
| 200AH | ADD | B       | A ← minuend + 9's comp subtrahend |
| 200BH | DAA |         | Decimal adjust |
| 200CH | JC | POSITIVE | If CY = 1, answer is positive |
| 200FH | CMA |         | Else, take 1's comp |
| 2010H | ADI | 01H     | Add 1 for 2's comp |
| 2012H | DAA |         | BCD adjust |
| 2013H | MVI | B,FFH   | B ← FFH (negative flag) |
| 2015H | POSITIVE: STA | 2052H | Store result |
| 2018H | HLT |         | Stop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** 10's complement BCD subtraction is the backbone of digital voltmeters and pump-flow totalizers where BCD counters are incremented/decremented in real time without binary-BCD conversion overhead.

## 3.8 Code Conversion — Hex to ASCII

The 8085 is often required to output hex digits to ASCII terminals (e.g., RS-232 to PC).

### Algorithm
1. Isolate the lower nibble (`ANI 0FH`).
2. Compare with 0AH.
3. If < 0AH, add 30H (ASCII '0' = 30H).
4. If ≥ 0AH, add 37H (so 0AH + 37H = 41H = 'A').

### Program Snippet
```assembly
    ANI 0FH      ; Mask upper nibble
    CPI 0AH      ; Compare with 10
    JC  NUM      ; If A < 10, jump
    ADI 07H      ; A ← A + 7 (makes range 10–15)
NUM: ADI 30H     ; A ← A + 30H (ASCII code)
    STA 2051H    ; Store ASCII byte
    HLT
```

> [!NOTE]
> Why **ADI 07H** before **ADI 30H**? Because ASCII 'A' = 41H and we want 0AH → 41H. If we just add 30H to 0AH, we get 3AH (':'), not 'A'. So we add 7 more, making 0AH + 37H = 41H. This is a frequent KTU viva question.

## 3.9 Step-by-Step Derivation of Bubble Sort Algorithm

For sorting N unsigned numbers in ascending order stored from 2051H onwards:

### Algorithm
1. Outer loop counter = N − 1.
2. Inner loop compares adjacent pairs.
3. If pair is out of order, swap via `MOV` + `XCHG` or stack operations.
4. Decrement counters; repeat.

### Program
```assembly
    LXI H, 2050H    ; HL → count
    MOV C, M        ; C = N
    DCR C           ; C = N − 1 (outer loop count)
OUTER: MOV D, C     ; D = inner loop count
    LXI H, 2051H    ; HL → first element
INNER: MOV A, M     ; A = current element
    INX H
    CMP M          ; Compare A with next
    JC SKIP        ; If A < next, no swap
    MOV B, M       ; B = next
    MOV M, A       ; Store current at next position
    DCX H
    MOV M, B       ; Store next at current position (SWAP done)
    INX H
SKIP: DCR D
    JNZ INNER
    DCR C
    JNZ OUTER
    HLT
```

### Algorithmic Complexity Analysis
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer iterations} &= N - 1 \\
\text{Inner iterations (avg)} &= \frac{N}{2} \\
\text{Total comparisons} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = O(N^2) \\
\text{Space complexity} &= O(1) \text{ (in-place sort)}
\end{aligned}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8085 Microprocessor Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Accumulator 8-bit ALU Operand A"] --> B["ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit 8-bit"]
    A --> F["Flag Register 5 status flags S Z AC P CY"]
    TMP["Temporary Register 8-bit"] --> B
    B --> A
    B --> F
    ID["Instruction Decoder 8-bit opcode input"] --> CU["Timing and Control Unit generates RD WR IO M status signals"]
    CU --> B
    RP["Register Array 6 x 8-bit B C D E H L plus pair logic"] --> B
    SP1["Stack Pointer 16-bit SP register"] --> RP
    PC1["Program Counter 16-bit PC register"] --> RP
    IDRP["Incrementer Decoder for PC increment"] --> PC1
    ABC["Address Bus 16-bit A0 to A15"] --> MEM["External Memory 64 KB addressable"]
    ABC --> IO["I O Devices via IN OUT instructions 8-bit"]
    DBC["Data Bus 8-bit bidirectional D0 to D7"] --> MEM
    DBC --> IO
    DBC --> B
    CU --> ABC
    CU --> DBC
    CLK["Clock Generator 6.144 MHz external divided to 3.072 MHz internal"] --> CU
    RST["Reset Circuit 5 reset pins TRAP RST7.5 RST6.5 RST5.5 INTR"] --> CU
```

## 4.2 Instruction Execution Cycle — Fetch-Decode-Execute Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start Power ON or Reset"] --> S2["PC holds 2000H next instruction address"]
    S2 --> S3["Fetch Opcode Read memory at PC into Instruction Register via Data Bus"]
    S3 --> S4["Decode Instruction Decoder interprets opcode field"]
    S4 --> S5{"Instruction Type"}
    S5 -->|Data Transfer| S6["Read source operand from register or memory into ALU bus"]
    S5 -->|Arithmetic Logical| S7["Perform operation in ALU update flags"]
    S5 -->|Branch Jump Call| S8["Modify PC with target address"]
    S5 -->|I O Machine Control| S9["Activate IO M RD WR pins as needed"]
    S6 --> S10["Write result to destination register or memory location"]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S11["Increment PC by opcode bytes already fetched"]
    S9 --> S12["Idle or Halt state"]
    S10 --> S13["Increment PC by 1 2 or 3 as per instruction length"]
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S14{"More Instructions"}
    S13 --> S14
    S14 -->|Yes| S3
    S14 -->|No HLT encountered| S15["Processor Halts waiting for interrupt"]
```

## 4.3 Bubble Sort Program Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST["Start Load N from 2050H"] --> A1["Initialize C equals N minus 1 outer counter"]
    A1 --> A2["Initialize D equals C inner counter"]
    A2 --> A3["Set HL pair to 2051H start of array"]
    A3 --> A4["Load A from memory M current element"]
    A4 --> A5["Increment HL to next element"]
    A5 --> A6["Compare A with M using CMP instruction"]
    A6 --> A7{"Is A less than M"}
    A7 -->|Yes| SK1["Skip swap and go to decrement D"]
    A7 -->|No| SW["Swap A and M using B as temporary register"]
    SW --> SK1
    SK1 --> A8["Decrement D"]
    A8 --> A9{"Is D zero"}
    A9 -->|No| A4
    A9 -->|Yes| A10["Decrement C"]
    A10 --> A11{"Is C zero"}
    A11 -->|No| A2
    A11 -->|Yes| END["Halt HLT instruction"]
```

## 4.4 8085 Microprocessor Kit — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR["Power Supply plus 5V plus 12V minus 5V minus 12V"] --> KIT["8085 Training Kit"]
    CPU["8085 CPU with Clock Reset Logic"] --> KIT
    EPROM["Monitor EPROM 0000H to 1FFFH"] --> KIT
    RAM["User RAM 2000H to FFFFH"] --> KIT
    KPD["Hex Keypad 16 keys for data and address input"] --> KIT
    DSP["7 Segment LED Display for address and data output"] --> KIT
    KIT --> LMP["Address Latches 74LS373 for A0 to A7 demultiplexed bus"]
    LMP --> XBD["External Expansion Bus for interfacing add on modules"]
    KIT --> SER["Serial Port RS232 for PC communication optional"]
```

## 4.5 Programmable Peripheral Interface (8255) Block Architecture (Optional Add-On)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["8085 CPU bus interface"] --> CTRL["Control Logic RD WR A0 A1 CS signals"]
    CTRL --> PG["Port A 8-bit PA0 to PA7"]
    CTRL --> PB["Port B 8-bit PB0 to PB7"]
    CTRL --> PC["Port C upper and lower 4-bit groups PC0 to PC7"]
    PG1["Group A Port A plus upper C"] --> PB1["Group B Port B plus lower C"]
    PG1 --> CTRL
    PB1 --> CTRL
    PG1 --> MODE["Mode 0 Simple I O Mode 1 Strobed I O Mode 2 Bidirectional"]
    PB1 --> MODE
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Best Practice Used Here:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`ST`, `A1`, `SW`, `PWR`, etc.), labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, ensuring clean Mermaid v10+ compilation.
