# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: List any three differences between the 8085 microprocessor and an 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | 8085 Microprocessor | 8051 Microcontroller |
|-------|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Definition | CPU only (needs external RAM/ROM) | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O on a single chip |
| 2 | Memory | External memory required (64 KB addressable) | Internal 4 KB ROM + 128 B RAM |
| 3 | I/O Ports | No internal I/O ports; uses I/O-mapped or memory-mapped I/O | Four 8-bit I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) |
| 4 | Applications | General-purpose computing, training kits | Embedded control (washing machines, pumps) |
| 5 | Instruction Set | 246 instructions, orthogonality limited | 255 instructions, mostly orthogonal |

*(Any three well-explained differences = 3 marks; 1 mark each)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: What is the function of the Program Counter (PC) and Stack Pointer (SP) in 8085? What is the size of each?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Program Counter (PC):** A 16-bit register that holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched. After each instruction fetch, PC is automatically incremented by the number of bytes in the instruction. Size = **16 bits**.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** A 16-bit register that points to the top of the stack in RAM. It is decremented on PUSH and incremented on POP. Size = **16 bits**.

*(1 mark for PC, 1 mark for SP, 1 mark for size + function)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Past Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8085 assembly language program to add two 16-bit numbers stored at memory locations **2050H–2051H** and **2052H–2053H**. Store the 16-bit result at **2054H–2055H** and the carry, if any, at **2056H**.

#### Model Solution

**Algorithm:**
1. Load low bytes into HL pair; load high bytes from second number into DE pair.
2. Add low bytes: `DAD D` adds DE to HL.
3. Store low byte of result.
4. If CY is set, increment high byte of result; else proceed.
5. Store high byte of result at 2055H.

**Assembly Program:**

| Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| 2000H | LHLD | 2050H   | HL ← M[2050H, 2051H] (16-bit first number) |
| 2003H | XCHG |         | DE ← HL (first number now in DE) |
| 2004H | LHLD | 2052H   | HL ← M[2052H, 2053H] (16-bit second number) |
| 2007H | DAD | D       | HL ← HL + DE (32-bit result in HL, CY in flag) |
| 2008H | SHLD | 2054H   | Store 16-bit result at 2054H–2055H |
| 200BH | MVI | A,00H   | A ← 0 |
| 200DH | JNC | STORE   | If no carry, store 00H |
| 2010H | MVI | A,01H   | A ← 1 (carry exists) |
| 2012H | STORE: STA | 2056H | Store carry at 2056H |
| 2015H | HLT |         | Stop |

**Memory Trace Example:**
- First number (2050H) = A5H, (2051H) = 3CH → 16-bit = 3CA5H
- Second number (2052H) = 7FH, (2053H) = 12H → 16-bit = 127FH
- Sum = 3CA5H + 127FH = 4F24H (CY = 0)
- Result (2054H) = 24H, (2055H) = 4FH, (2056H) = 00H

*(4 marks algorithm, 3 marks program = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the 8085 **flag register** in detail with a neat diagram. List the instructions that affect the **carry flag**.

#### Model Solution

The 8085 flag register (PSW) is an 8-bit register where only 5 bits are used as status indicators:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSW bits:} \quad & D_7=S, \; D_6=Z, \; D_5=0, \; D_4=AC, \; D_3=0, \; D_2=P, \; D_1=1, \; D_0=CY
\end{aligned}
$$

| Flag | Meaning | Set When |
|------|---------|----------|
| S (Sign) | Result is negative | MSB of result = 1 |
| Z (Zero) | Result is zero | All 8 bits of result = 0 |
| AC (Aux Carry) | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 | Used in BCD arithmetic |
| P (Parity) | Even number of 1s in result | Used for error detection |
| CY (Carry) | Carry out of MSB | Used in multi-byte arithmetic |

**Instructions that affect CY:**
1. **ADD, ADI** — Set CY on carry out of bit 7.
2. **SUB, SUI** — Set CY if borrow needed (equivalent to setting CY if A < operand).
3. **CMP, CPI** — Performs A − operand, sets CY accordingly.
4. **DAD** — Sets CY if carry from bit 15 (only instruction that uses CY as a destination for 16-bit add).
5. **STC, CMC** — Directly set/complement CY.
6. **RRC, RAR, RLC, RAL** — Rotate instructions shift CY into the data path.

**Instructions that do NOT affect CY:**
- INR, DCR, INX, DCX
- MOV, MVI, LXI
- ANA, ORA, XRA (and their immediate variants) — clear CY to 0

*(3 marks diagram + table, 2 marks CY-affecting list, 2 marks CY-not-affecting list = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B-B `[Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8085 ALP to find the **sum of squares** of the first **N** natural numbers. N is stored at **2050H**; store the 16-bit result at **2051H–2052H**.

#### Model Solution

**Mathematical Identity:**
$$
S = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + \dots + N^2 = \frac{N(N+1)(2N+1)}{6}
$$

**Algorithm (Direct Summation Method):**
1. Load N into register C (counter).
2. Initialize HL pair for result (HL = 0).
3. Load counter value, multiply by itself (using repeated addition), add to HL.
4. Decrement counter; repeat until 0.
5. Store 16-bit result.

**Assembly Program:**

| Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Comment |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| 2000H | LXI | H,2050H | HL points to N |
| 2003H | MOV | C,M     | C ← N (counter) |
| 2004H | LXI | H,0000H | HL ← 0 (running sum) |
| 2007H | LOOP: MOV | A,C   | A ← current counter |
| 2009H | MOV | D,A     | D ← counter (for multiplication) |
| 200AH | MVI | E,00H   | E ← 0 (product accumulator, low) |
| 200CH | MVI | B,00H   | B ← 0 (product accumulator, high) |
| 200EH | MULT: MOV | A,E   | A ← E (current product) |
| 2010H | ADD | D       | A ← E + counter |
| 2011H | MOV | E,A     | E ← A |
| 2012H | JNC | NOCARRY | If no carry, skip |
| 2015H | INR | B       | B ← B + 1 (propagate carry) |
| 2016H | NOCARRY: DCR | D   | D ← D − 1 |
| 2018H | JNZ | MULT    | Repeat multiplication |
| 201BH | DAD | D       | HL ← HL + DE (add square to sum) |
| 201CH | DCR | C       | C ← C − 1 |
| 201DH | JNZ | LOOP    | Loop back |
| 2020H | SHLD | 2051H  | Store 16-bit sum |
| 2023H | HLT |         | Stop |

**Verification for N = 3:**
$$
S = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 = 14 = \text{0E}_{16}
$$

*(5 marks program, 2 marks algorithm/verification = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain how the 8085 executes a **CALL–RET** subroutine sequence. What is the role of the stack?

#### Model Solution

**Call–Ret Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Main program executes linearly until CALL address encountered"] --> B["Push PC high byte onto stack SP decrements and writes high byte"]
    B --> C["Push PC low byte onto stack SP decrements and writes low byte"]
    C --> D["Load PC with target subroutine address from CALL operand"]
    D --> E["Subroutine executes from entry address until RET instruction encountered"]
    E --> F["POP low byte from stack into PC low SP increments"]
    F --> G["POP high byte from stack into PC high SP increments"]
    G --> H["Main program resumes from instruction after original CALL"]
```

**Role of the Stack:**
1. **Return Address Storage** — The CALL instruction needs to remember where to return after the subroutine finishes. Since the 8085 has no dedicated hardware for this (unlike some microcontrollers with link registers), it uses the RAM stack.
2. **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) Mechanism** — Nested subroutine calls (a CALL inside another CALL) work correctly because the most recently pushed return address is the first one to be popped on RET.
3. **Parameter Passing** — Subroutine parameters can be passed via the stack in more advanced programs.
4. **Local Variable Storage** — Subroutines can use the stack to store temporary variables without conflicting with main program variables.

**Register Changes During CALL 2050H (when current PC = 2010H, assuming current PC is 2013H after fetching 3-byte CALL):**
- Memory[SP − 1] ← PC_high = 20H (SP = SP − 1)
- Memory[SP − 2] ← PC_low = 13H (SP = SP − 2)
- PC ← 2050H (new value)

*(3 marks flowchart, 2 marks stack role, 2 marks register/PC trace = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in 8085 ALP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `DAA` after BCD arithmetic (–2 marks):** The Decimal Adjust Accumulator must follow every ADD/SUB/ADI/SUI on BCD data. Without it, results are binary, not BCD.
>
> 2. **Using `SUB` instead of `CMP` (–1 mark):** Both set flags, but CMP preserves the operand — critical when the original value is needed later.
>
> 3. **Confusing PUSH/POP order (–2 marks):** PUSH BC writes C first (at SP − 1), then B (at SP − 2). POP BC reads B first (from SP), then C (from SP + 1). Wrong order corrupts both registers.
>
> 4. **Missing `HLT` at end of program (–1 mark):** Many kits execute the next memory contents (junk) after your program ends, leading to a "program crashed" remark in the lab record.
>
> 5. **Wrong addressing in `LXI H, 2050H` (–1 mark):** Students sometimes write `LXI H, 2050` — the assembler will treat it as `LXI H, 07E4H` (decimal 2050 = hex 0802!). Always write `H` suffix for hex.
>
> 6. **Not checking CY after DAD (–2 marks):** The DAD instruction (16-bit add) is the **only** arithmetic instruction that uses the Carry flag as a result, not an input. Forgetting to check CY after DAD loses carry information.
>
> 7. **Stack underflow in PUSH (–3 marks):** If SP is already at 2000H (lowest RAM) and PUSH is executed, the kit may "hang" or corrupt monitor code. Always initialize SP to a safe value (e.g., `LXI SP, FFFFH` at the start).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ultimate 8085 ALP Quick-Reference Card for KTU Lab Exam**

### Hardware Essentials
- [ ] **8085 = 8-bit data, 16-bit address, 64 KB memory, 3.072 MHz internal clock**
- [ ] **7 × 8-bit registers (A, B, C, D, E, H, L), 3 × 16-bit pairs (BC, DE, HL)**
- [ ] **5 status flags: S, Z, AC, P, CY** (D5 = 0, D3 = 0, D1 = 1 always)
- [ ] **Reset vector = 0000H; typical user program start = 2000H**

### Instruction Categories
- [ ] **Data Transfer:** MOV, MVI, LXI, LDA, STA, LHLD, SHLD, XCHG, PUSH, POP
- [ ] **Arithmetic:** ADD, ADI, SUB, SUI, INR, DCR, INX, DCX, DAD
- [ ] **Logical:** ANA, ANI, ORA, ORI, XRA, XRI, CMA, CMP, CPI
- [ ] **Branching:** JMP (unconditional), JZ/JNZ/JC/JNC/JP/JM/JPE/JPO (conditional), CALL, RET
- [ ] **I/O & Control:** IN, OUT, HLT, NOP, EI, DI, RIM, SIM, RST n

### Key Conversions
- [ ] **2's complement = 1's complement + 1** (used for subtraction)
- [ ] **9's complement BCD subtract** = CMA + ADI 01H + DAA
- [ ] **Hex to ASCII:** If < 0AH → ADD 30H; If ≥ 0AH → ADD 37H
- [ ] **BCD to Binary:** Pack two BCD digits → use DAA after ADD/SUB

### Program Debugging Tips
- [ ] Use **single-step execution** (EXEC key then STEP) to trace instruction-by-instruction.
- [ ] Watch the **address and data displays** at every step.
- [ ] Use the **REG (register) key** to inspect A, B, C, D, E, H, L, F, PC, SP contents.
- [ ] For errors, dump the program memory using **DUMP** command from address 2000H.

### Exam Day Formula
- [ ] **Cycle time** = 1 / 3.072 MHz = **0.325 µs = 325 ns** per T-state
- [ ] **Memory cycles** of 8085: opcode fetch = 4T, memory read = 3T, memory write = 3T, I/O = 3T
- [ ] **Instruction execution time** = T-states × 0.325 µs

### Most Frequently Asked Programs in KTU Labs
- [ ] ✅ 8-bit addition/subtraction of two numbers
- [ ] ✅ Sum of N numbers in an array
- [ ] ✅ Find largest/smallest of N numbers
- [ ] ✅ Ascending/descending sort (Bubble sort)
- [ ] ✅ BCD addition and 10's complement subtraction
- [ ] ✅ Hex to ASCII and ASCII to Hex conversion
- [ ] ✅ Multiplication by repeated addition
- [ ] ✅ Division by repeated subtraction
- [ ] ✅ 2's complement of a number
- [ ] ✅ Block transfer of data

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Testing of Pelton Turbine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Testing of Pelton Turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pelton Turbine (Pelton Wheel):** A tangential-flow **impulse turbine** in which a high-velocity jet of water, issuing from a nozzle, strikes the double-cup shaped buckets fixed on the periphery of a runner and converts the kinetic energy of the jet into rotational mechanical work. It is named after American inventor **Lester Allan Pelton (1880)** and is the standard prime mover for **high-head (above ~300 m)**, **low-discharge** hydroelectric power plants.

### 1.2 Working Principle – The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a child holding a cup of water and dropping it onto a horizontal spinning paddle wheel. The water "pushes" the paddle to make it turn. The Pelton turbine works exactly like this — except the water is accelerated through a precisely shaped **nozzle** (acting like a thumb covering the hose to form a fast jet) and the paddles are specially curved **double-buckets** that scoop the jet, turn it through nearly **180°**, and eject it sideways. This near-reversal of the jet's direction extracts maximum momentum.

### 1.3 Salient Features (KTU-Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Turbine type:** Tangential impulse turbine (single or multi-jet).
> - **Head range:** 30 m to 1800 m (best suited for high heads).
> - **Specific speed range:** $N_s = 10$ to $35$ (SI, metric units) — the **lowest** among all turbines.
> - **Number of buckets:** $Z = 15 + \dfrac{D}{2d_j}$ (empirical formula), where $D$ is runner diameter and $d_j$ is jet diameter.
> - **Governing:** By adjusting the **spear/needle stroke** inside the nozzle.
> - **Standard runner to jet ratio:** $D/d_j \approx 12$ to $20$.
> - **Pressure at runner:** Atmospheric on both sides of the bucket (pure impulse action).

### 1.4 Components of the Standard Test Rig

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Turbine (Pelton runner)** | The rotor on which buckets are mounted. |
| **Nozzle + Spear / Needle** | Converts pressure head into kinetic head and regulates discharge. |
| **Deflector (Deflecting Plate)** | Diverts the jet away from buckets during load rejection to prevent runaway. |
| **Brake Drum & Rope Brake** | Applies tangential load to measure shaft power output. |
| **Spring Balance / Load Cell** | Reads the net braking force $F = (W_1 - W_2)$ kgf. |
| **Pressure Gauge** | Reads gross head $H$ at the nozzle inlet. |
| **Tachometer (digital/analog)** | Measures runner speed $N$ in **rpm**. |
| **Stop Watch + Measuring Tank** | Measures discharge $Q$ via volumetric collection. |
| **Inlet / Outlet Tanks** | Maintain a constant supply and steady head. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Energy Conversion at Nozzle:** The pressure head $H$ at the inlet of the nozzle is converted into kinetic energy of the jet. By Bernoulli's equation (with nozzle coefficient $C_v$):
   $$V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$$
   Here $V_1$ is the absolute jet velocity, $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$, and $H$ is the **gross head** in metres.

2. **Bucket Striking:** The jet strikes the splitter ridge of the bucket and divides into two halves, each turning through angle $\beta$ (typically $165°$ to $170°$). Energy is transferred by change of momentum.

3. **Velocity Triangle Construction (Stationary frame):**
   - At **inlet**: Absolute velocity = $V_1$ (purely tangential), so $V_{w1} = V_1$ and $V_{f1} = 0$.
   - At **outlet**: The whirl component is reduced because the water leaves with a residual whirl velocity $V_{w2}$.

4. **Runner / Bucket Velocity:**
   $$u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$
   where $D$ = mean bucket-circle diameter (m), $N$ = shaft speed (rpm).

5. **Bucket Friction:** The relative velocity at exit is less than at inlet due to friction on the bucket surface:
   $$V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1} = K(V_1 - u)$$
   where $K$ is the **bucket friction coefficient** ($0.85 \leq K \leq 0.98$).

6. **Outlet Whirl Velocity (using outlet triangle):**
   $$V_{w2} = u - V_{r2} \cos\beta = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$

7. **Work Done Per Second (Euler's Equation for Impulse Turbine):**
   $$W = \rho \, a \, V_1 \, u \, (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$$
   Since both $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ act in the same direction for an impulse wheel, we use the **subtractive** form:
   $$W = \rho \, a \, V_1 \, u \, (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$$

8. **Condition for Maximum Efficiency:**
   Differentiating with respect to $u$ and setting to zero gives:
   $$\boxed{u = \frac{V_1}{2}}$$
   This is the famous **"half-jet rule"** — bucket speed equals half the jet speed.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — All formulas for Pelton Turbine Performance Test**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s; $C_v = 0.96$ to $0.99$ |
| 2 | Bucket velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Speed ratio | $K_u = \dfrac{u}{V_1} = \dfrac{u}{\sqrt{2gH}}$ | dimensionless, optimum $\approx 0.46$ |
| 4 | Relative velocity in | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ | m/s |
| 5 | Relative velocity out | $V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1}$ | m/s; $K = 0.85$–$0.98$ |
| 6 | Outlet whirl velocity | $V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$ | m/s |
| 7 | Work done / sec | $W = \rho a V_1 u (V_1 - V_{w2})$ | Watts |
| 8 | Jet area | $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_j^{\,2}$ | m$^2$ |
| 9 | Mass flow rate | $\dot m = \rho a V_1$ | kg/s |
| 10 | Shaft power output | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W; $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R_{eff} \cdot g$ |
| 11 | Water power (input) | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | W |
| 12 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1 - V_{w2}}{V_1}$ | fraction |
| 13 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{W}$ | fraction |
| 14 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_h \times \eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}}$ | fraction |
| 15 | Maximum efficiency | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1+K\cos\beta}{2}$ | when $u = V_1/2$ |
| 16 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm-metric HP-m |
| 17 | Number of buckets | $Z = 15 + \dfrac{D}{2 d_j}$ | empirical (Glick's formula) |

### 2.3 Engineering Significance

Pelton wheels dominate **run-of-the-mountains**, **mini-hydro**, and **pumped-storage** plants. Their mechanical simplicity (no casing pressure, no draft tube, atmospheric exit) makes them the **most efficient** water turbine class, routinely achieving **90%–92% overall efficiency** at design load — the highest of any prime mover in existence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work Done per Second and Hydraulic Efficiency

**Given data at the bucket:**
- Absolute jet velocity at inlet = $V_1$ (purely tangential).
- Bucket velocity = $u$.
- Relative velocity at inlet = $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ (because $V_{f1} = 0$).
- Bucket exit angle = $\beta$.
- Friction coefficient of bucket = $K$.

**Step 1 — Mass striking the runner per second:**
The volume of water striking per second = jet area $\times$ relative velocity (because the bucket is moving against the jet).

$$\dot m = \rho \cdot a \cdot V_{r1} = \rho a (V_1 - u)$$

> *(Note: the relative velocity, NOT the absolute jet velocity, governs how fast water actually meets the bucket, which is why we use $V_{r1}$.)*

**Step 2 — Change in tangential momentum (Newton's II law):**
Initial tangential (whirl) velocity of water = $V_1$.
Final tangential (whirl) velocity of water = $V_{w2}$.

Force on water by bucket (opposing motion):
$$F_{water} = \dot m (V_1 - V_{w2})$$

By Newton's third law, the equal and opposite force on the bucket is what does the work:
$$F_{bucket} = \dot m (V_1 - V_{w2})$$

**Step 3 — Work done per second (Force × Velocity):**
$$W = F_{bucket} \times u = \rho a (V_1 - u) \cdot u \cdot (V_1 - V_{w2})$$

**Step 4 — Express $V_{w2}$ using the outlet velocity triangle:**

From the outlet triangle, the relative velocity $V_{r2}$ makes angle $\beta$ with the tangential direction. Hence:

$$V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$

Substituting:
$$W = \rho a (V_1 - u) \, u \, \big[(V_1 - u) + K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta\big]$$
$$W = \rho a \, u \, (V_1 - u)^2 \, (1 + K\cos\beta)$$

This is the famous general expression for the work extracted by a Pelton wheel.

**Step 5 — Condition for maximum work (and hence max efficiency):**
Treat $V_1$ and $K$ as constants. Differentiate $W$ with respect to $u$:

$$\dfrac{dW}{du} = \rho a (V_1 - u)\big[(1 + K\cos\beta)(V_1 - 2u)\big] = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution, the second factor must vanish:

$$V_1 - 2u = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency:**
Hydraulic efficiency = Work done per unit weight ÷ KE per unit weight:

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1 - V_{w2}}{V_1}$$

Substituting $V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$ and $u = V_1/2$:

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1 - \big[\frac{V_1}{2} - K\big(\frac{V_1}{2}\big)\cos\beta\big]}{V_1}$$

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1\big[1 - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{K\cos\beta}{2}\big]}{V_1} = \dfrac{1 + K\cos\beta}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 + K\cos\beta}{2}}$$

**Special case —** For a plain single-bucket with $\beta = 0°$ (rare) and $K = 1$:
$$\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 + 1}{2} = 1 \;\; (100\%)$$

For a **split (double) bucket** with $\beta = 165°$:
$$\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9 \cdot \cos(165°)}{2} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9 \cdot (-0.966)}{2} \approx 0.065$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A split bucket with $\beta = 165°$ gives a small NEGATIVE $V_{w2}$ — the water actually leaves with a small forward whirl, dramatically reducing kinetic energy lost in the exit jet and increasing efficiency well above the no-split case. This is why every modern Pelton bucket has a central splitter ridge.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A Pelton wheel has a mean runner diameter of $1.2$ m and operates at $500$ rpm. The net head available is $400$ m, the jet diameter is $10$ cm, $C_v = 0.97$, $K = 0.95$, and the bucket exit angle is $165°$. Calculate the power developed and the hydraulic efficiency.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bucket velocity:**
$$u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1.2 \cdot 500}{60} = \pi \cdot 10 = 31.416 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Jet velocity:**
$$V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 400}$$
$$V_1 = 0.97 \cdot \sqrt{7848} = 0.97 \cdot 88.59 = 85.93 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Speed ratio check (should be ~0.46):**
$$K_u = \dfrac{u}{V_1} = \dfrac{31.416}{85.93} = 0.366$$

Since $K_u \neq 0.5$, this is **not** the optimum design point. We must compute actual efficiency.

**Step 4 — Outlet whirl velocity:**
$$V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$
$$V_{w2} = 31.416 - 0.95(85.93 - 31.416)\cos(165°)$$
$$V_{w2} = 31.416 - 0.95 \cdot 54.514 \cdot (-0.9659)$$
$$V_{w2} = 31.416 + 50.029 = 81.445 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic Efficiency:**
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1 - V_{w2}}{V_1} = \dfrac{85.93 - 81.445}{85.93} = \dfrac{4.485}{85.93} = 0.0522$$
$$\eta_h \approx 5.22\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why so low?** The speed ratio is wrong — the wheel is grossly underspeed for this head. Re-design at $u = V_1/2 = 42.96$ m/s would give the textbook $\eta_h = \dfrac{1 + 0.95 \cdot \cos(165°)}{2} \approx 86.7\%$.

**Step 6 — Jet area and mass flow:**
$$a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2$$
$$\dot m = \rho a V_1 = 1000 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 85.93 = 674.96\;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 7 — Power developed:**
$$W = \rho a u (V_1 - u)^2 (1 + K\cos\beta)$$
$$W = 674.96 \cdot 31.416 \cdot (54.514)^2 \cdot (1 + 0.95\cdot(-0.9659))$$
$$W = 674.96 \cdot 31.416 \cdot 2971.78 \cdot 0.08239$$
$$W \approx 5.22 \times 10^6 \;\text{W} = 5.22\;\text{MW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Tank\nConstant Head H] --> B[Inlet Pipe\nwith Venturi Meter]
    B --> C[Pressure Gauge\nReads H at Nozzle Inlet]
    C --> D[Nozzle + Spear Valve\nDischarge Q controlled]
    D --> E[Jet of Water\nVelocity V1 = Cv*sqrt2gH]
    E --> F[Pelton Runner with Buckets\nDiameter D, Speed N rpm]
    F --> G[Shaft]
    G --> H[Brake Drum\nRope + Spring Balance]
    H --> I[Tachometer\nReads N]
    H --> J[Spring Balance Reads\nW1 and W2 kgf]
    F --> K[Tailrace\nReturns water to sump]
    H --> L[Output Power\nPshaft = 2*pi*N*T/60]

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style H fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style L fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Data Acquisition Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Open inlet valve slowly] --> P2[Allow steady flow at full load]
    P2 --> P3[Read head H from pressure gauge]
    P3 --> P4[Read speed N from tachometer]
    P4 --> P5[Measure discharge Q\nusing measuring tank + stopwatch]
    P5 --> P6[Read spring balance loads W1 and W2]
    P6 --> P7[Apply load by tightening rope brake]
    P7 --> P8[Wait for steady state]
    P8 --> P9[Record new H, N, Q, W1, W2]
    P9 --> P10{More load points?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P7
    P10 -- No --> P11[Compute efficiencies and power]
    P11 --> P12[Plot curves:\nNs vs eta, N vs Q, P vs Q]
    P12 --> P13[End of Test]

    style P1 fill:#d1e7dd
    style P6 fill:#fff3cd
    style P11 fill:#cfe2ff
    style P13 fill:#f8d7da
```

### 4.3 Block Schematic — Energy Conversion Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Pressure Energy\nrho*g*H] -->|Nozzle| S2[Kinetic Energy\n0.5*rho*V1^2]
    S2 -->|Bucket impact| S3[Mechanical Energy\nTorque*T on shaft]
    S3 -->|Brake Drum| S4[Heat Energy\nAt rope friction]

    S1 -.- L1[Losses: nozzle,\npipe friction]:::loss
    S2 -.- L2[Losses: bucket\nfriction, exit KE]:::loss
    S3 -.- L3[Losses: bearing,\nwindage, rope slip]:::loss

    classDef loss fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

### 4.4 Physical Setup Reference (Text Schematic)

```
                       ┌──[Pressure Gauge H]
                       │
   ┌───────┐      ╔════════╗      ┌────────────────┐
   │ Sump  │──┐   ║ NOZZLE ║ ──▶  │   JET  V1     │
   │ Tank  │  │   ║ +SPEAR ║      └───────┬────────┘
   └───────┘  │   ╚════════╝              │
              │        │                  ▼
              │   [Venturi]        ┌─────────────┐
              │    Meter            │  P E L T O N│
              │                     │   RUNNER    │──┬─▶ Tachometer
              │                     │  Buckets    │  │
              │                     │  D, N rpm   │  │
              │                     └──────┬──────┘  │
              │                            │         │
              │                       [BRAKE DRUM]   │
              │                            │         │
              │                  [Spring Balance]────┘
              │                     W1, W2 kgf
              │
              ▼
        [Measuring Tank]
         + Stop Watch → Q
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*List the assumptions made while deriving the velocity triangles of a Pelton wheel bucket.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The jet has a uniform velocity $V_1$ over its cross-section (no contraction / velocity distribution).
2. The bucket surface is frictionless — but in practice a friction coefficient $K$ is introduced ($V_{r2} = K V_{r1}$).
3. The jet strikes the bucket tangentially so that $V_{f1} = 0$ and the inlet whirl velocity equals the jet velocity ($V_{w1} = V_1$).
4. Friction of the surrounding air on the jet and runner is negligible.
5. The pressure of water all along the flow is constant and equal to atmospheric (pure impulse, no pressure drop across bucket).

*(Each correct assumption with one-line explanation earns 1 mark; full marks for 3 well-explained points.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

*What is a "split bucket" in a Pelton turbine? Why is it preferred over a single-curve bucket?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **split bucket** (or double-cup bucket) is one in which a central **splitter ridge** is provided at the centre of the bucket, dividing the incoming jet into two equal halves that flow along two symmetric curved paths.

**Reasons for preference:**
- It halves the relative velocity to be turned in each half-bucket, **reducing hydraulic losses** and bucket wear.
- It produces a **negative exit whirl component** ($V_{w2}$ becomes small and negative), thereby **increasing hydraulic efficiency** well above that of a single-curve bucket of the same exit angle.
- It prevents the jet from overshooting the bucket at high speeds and provides smoother flow.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for any two reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done per second on a Pelton wheel in terms of jet velocity $V_1$, bucket velocity $u$, bucket angle $\beta$, and bucket friction coefficient $K$. State the condition for maximum efficiency.  **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Mass flow rate striking the runner** = $\dot m = \rho a (V_1 - u)$, where $a$ is jet area. *(1 mark)*
2. **Initial whirl velocity** = $V_1$; **final whirl velocity** = $V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$. *(2 marks)*
3. **Force on bucket by water** (Newton's II law) = $F = \dot m (V_1 - V_{w2})$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Work done per second** = $F \times u$
   $$W = \rho a (V_1 - u) \cdot u \cdot (V_1 - V_{w2}) = \rho a u (V_1 - u)^2 (1 + K\cos\beta)$$ *(2 marks)*
5. **Maximum work condition** by $\frac{dW}{du}=0$ gives $u = V_1/2$. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** A Pelton wheel has a mean bucket-circle diameter of $1.8$ m and runs at $300$ rpm. The net head is $300$ m, jet diameter is $15$ cm, coefficient of velocity $C_v = 0.98$, friction coefficient $K = 0.95$, and the bucket exit angle is $165°$. Determine:
   (i) Power developed by the runner
   (ii) Hydraulic efficiency
   (iii) Specific speed  **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bucket velocity:**
$$u = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1.8 \cdot 300}{60} = 28.27\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Jet velocity:**
$$V_1 = 0.98 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 300} = 0.98 \cdot 76.72 = 75.18\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Jet area:**
$$a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Outlet whirl velocity:**
$$V_{w2} = 28.27 - 0.95 \cdot (75.18 - 28.27) \cdot \cos(165°)$$
$$V_{w2} = 28.27 - 0.95 \cdot 46.91 \cdot (-0.9659) = 28.27 + 43.05 = 71.32\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — (i) Power developed:**
$$W = 1000 \cdot 0.01767 \cdot 75.18 \cdot 28.27 \cdot (75.18 - 71.32)$$
$$W = 1328.5 \cdot 28.27 \cdot 3.86 \approx 1.449 \times 10^5\;\text{W} \approx 144.9\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — (ii) Hydraulic efficiency:**
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1 - V_{w2}}{V_1} = \dfrac{75.18 - 71.32}{75.18} = \dfrac{3.86}{75.18} = 0.0513 \approx 5.13\%$$

**Step 7 — (iii) Specific speed (in SI units, metric):**
Discharge $Q = a V_1 = 0.01767 \times 75.18 = 1.328\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
$$N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \dfrac{300\sqrt{195}}{300^{5/4}} = \dfrac{300 \cdot 13.96}{72.08} \approx 58.1\;\text{(SI metric)}$$

*(The low $N_s$ confirms Pelton is suitable for high heads — 1 mark each for steps 1, 2, 5; 2 marks for steps 4 and 6 combined.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of inlet and outlet velocity triangles, derive the expression for the **maximum hydraulic efficiency** of a Pelton wheel. Show that the maximum efficiency occurs when the bucket velocity is half the jet velocity. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Inlet triangle:** $V_1$ is tangential, $u$ is bucket velocity, $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ acts at angle $90°$ to $V_1$. Hence $V_{w1} = V_1$ and $V_{f1} = 0$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Outlet triangle:** $V_{r2} = K(V_1 - u)$ at angle $\beta$ with the tangential direction. Therefore $V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$. *(2 marks)*
3. **Hydraulic efficiency:**
   $$\eta_h = \dfrac{V_1 - V_{w2}}{V_1} = \dfrac{(V_1 - u) + K(V_1 - u)\cos\beta}{V_1} = \dfrac{(V_1 - u)(1 + K\cos\beta)}{V_1}$$ *(2 marks)*
4. **Differentiation:** Treating $V_1, K, \beta$ as constants,
   $$\dfrac{d\eta_h}{du} = \dfrac{-(1 + K\cos\beta)}{V_1} = 0 \;\;\text{is impossible — we differentiate } W$$
   Alternatively, since $W \propto (V_1-u)u$, maximum when $u = V_1/2$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Maximum hydraulic efficiency:**
   $$\boxed{\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 + K\cos\beta}{2}}$$ *(1 mark)*

**(b)** In a Pelton wheel test, the following observations were recorded:

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Net head, $H$ | $250$ m |
| Jet diameter, $d_j$ | $12$ cm |
| Speed, $N$ | $450$ rpm |
| Mean runner diameter, $D$ | $1.5$ m |
| Discharge, $Q$ | $0.95$ m³/s |
| Brake drum diameter | $40$ cm |
| Spring balance readings | $W_1 = 40$ kgf, $W_2 = 4$ kgf |

Calculate the **shaft power**, **water power**, and **overall efficiency** of the turbine. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net load on brake drum:**
$$F_{net} = W_1 - W_2 = 40 - 4 = 36\;\text{kgf}$$

**Step 2 — Effective radius:**
$$R = \dfrac{0.40}{2} = 0.20\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Torque on shaft:**
$$T = F_{net} \cdot g \cdot R = 36 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.20 = 70.632\;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft power output:**
$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 450 \cdot 70.632}{60} = 3328.4\;\text{W} \approx 3.33\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Water (input) power:**
$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 250 = 2\,329\,875\;\text{W} \approx 2329.9\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Overall efficiency:**
$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{3328.4}{2\,329\,875} = 1.43 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.143\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit trap for spring balance:** Many students compute $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R$ **without** multiplying by $g$ — this gives the wrong SI unit (kgf·m instead of N·m). Always remember: $1$ kgf $= 9.81$ N. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing gross and net head:** $H$ read from the pressure gauge is at the nozzle inlet — already includes pipe friction, so use it directly as net head $H$. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Brake drum radius:** A common error is using diameter instead of radius. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting $\pi$ in shaft power formula:** $P = 2\pi NT/60$ — students often write $2NT/60$. **(–2 marks)**
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer:** Always state $\text{kW}$ or $\text{W}$ explicitly. **(–1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **🔥 MUST-REMEMBER POINTS FOR KTU EXAMS 🔥**

- ☐ **Pelton = Impulse = Pressure atmospheric throughout the runner** (no draft tube needed).
- ☐ **Jet velocity:** $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$, with $C_v \approx 0.97$–$0.99$.
- ☐ **Bucket velocity:** $u = \pi DN/60$.
- ☐ **Optimum speed ratio:** $u/V_1 = 0.46$ (for split bucket, $\beta = 165°$).
- ☐ **For maximum efficiency:** $u = V_1/2$ (rigorous proof) and $\eta_{\max} = (1 + K\cos\beta)/2$.
- ☐ **Number of buckets:** $Z = 15 + D/(2d_j)$ — Glick's empirical formula.
- ☐ **Governing mechanism:** Spear/needle valve (NOT guide vanes like in Francis/Kaplan).
- ☐ **Safety device:** Deflector plate + relief valve — to handle load rejection without runaway.
- ☐ **For $\beta = 0$ plain bucket:** $V_{w2} = u - K(V_1 - u)$ and $\eta_{\max} = (1+K)/2$.
- ☐ **For split bucket ($\beta = 165°$):** $V_{w2}$ may become negative — increases efficiency dramatically.
- ☐ **Power formulas:** $P_{shaft} = 2\pi NT/60$ where $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R \cdot g$.
- ☐ **Water power:** $P_w = \rho g Q H$ (NEVER forget the factor $g$).
- ☐ **Specific speed:** $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ in (rpm, kW, m) — ranges 10–35 for Pelton.
- ☐ **Three losses:** Nozzle ($C_v < 1$), bucket friction ($K < 1$), mechanical (bearings + windage).
- ☐ **Typical characteristic curves to plot in lab:** $N$ vs $Q$, $\eta_h$ vs $Q$, $P$ vs $Q$, $\eta_{overall}$ vs $Q$.
- ☐ **Open flume vs closed pipe system:** Pelton test rigs usually use a closed pipe with pressure-gauge head measurement.
- ☐ **Always subtract rope-friction zero load:** $W_{2,\text{zero}}$ is non-zero due to rope stiffness; subtract it.
- ☐ **Units audit at the END of every numerical:** Convert N to kN, W to kW, m to cm when required.
- ☐ **Remember:** "Bucket speed equals half the jet speed" is the **design mantra** — write it prominently in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Testing of Francis/Kaplan Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Testing of Francis/Kaplan Turbines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Performance Testing of Francis/Kaplan Turbines** is the standardized experimental procedure used in hydraulic machinery laboratories to evaluate the operational behavior of **reaction turbines** (Francis and Kaplan types) under varying load conditions at a constant rated head and constant rated speed. The test produces graphical relationships — known as **characteristic curves** — that express how discharge ($Q$), shaft power ($P$), and **hydraulic efficiency ($\eta_h$)** vary with each other and with the **synchronising speed ($N$)**, allowing selection of the *Best Efficiency Point (BEP)*, the *Normal Speed Line*, and the *Limiting (Throat) Condition* of the turbine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> A **Reaction Turbine** is one in which the working fluid undergoes a *pressure drop* across the runner, and the fluid leaves the runner at a pressure *lower* than the inlet pressure. The pressure energy is converted into kinetic energy *both* in the guide vanes and within the runner itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **cyclist pedaling a bicycle** up a hill (head, $H$):

- The **Francis turbine** is like a **multi-speed geared bicycle** suited for medium hills with moderate flow. The water enters the runner *radially* and leaves *axially* — a "mixed flow" design.
- The **Kaplan turbine** is like a **motorcycle with adjustable blades** for steep, fast-flowing rivers. The water flows *axially* through the runner, and the runner blades **rotate** to match changing flow conditions — just like swiveling fan blades.

**Performance testing** is exactly like taking your bicycle/motorcycle to a test track:
1. You hold the gear/route (head) constant.
2. You apply different "throttles" (discharge / load).
3. You measure how much "work" you get out (shaft power) for a given input (hydraulic power).
4. You plot efficiency vs. load to find your *sweet spot*.

> [!NOTE]
> The graphical family of curves produced — Head vs. Discharge, Efficiency vs. Discharge, Power vs. Discharge, Speed vs. Discharge, and Iso-Efficiency (Mushel) curves — is collectively called the **Complete Characteristic of the Turbine**. For KTU, remember the **Three Primary Curves** (Head–Discharge, Efficiency–Discharge, Power–Discharge) and the **Iso-Efficiency (Constant Efficiency) Curves**.

### 1.3 Classification Context

| Turbine Type | Flow Direction | Specific Speed Range ($N_s$) | Typical Head Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Francis** | Mixed (Radial → Axial) | 60 – 400 (metric) | 30 m – 600 m |
| **Kaplan** | Axial | 400 – 1000+ (metric) | 2 m – 70 m |

Specific speed formula (metric):
$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

Where $P$ is in **kW** and $H$ is in **metres**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Performance Testing?

Performance testing is mandated in KTU's PCPEL508 lab syllabus because it answers the **Three Engineering Questions** every hydraulic engineer must solve before installing a turbine:

1. **How much power** will the turbine deliver at the design head?
2. **What is the peak efficiency**, and at what discharge does it occur?
3. **What are the safe operating limits** to avoid cavitation, vibration, and runaway?

The test typically fixes $H$ and $N$ and varies the **brake load** (mechanical load on the dynamometer/generator), which causes $Q$ to vary and allows plotting of curves.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Test Logic

- **Step 1 — Fix Head ($H$):** Maintain a constant gross head by holding the upstream tank level steady.
- **Step 2 — Fix Speed ($N$):** Maintain synchronising speed (typically 1500 rpm for 50 Hz, 2-pole alternator in KTU labs).
- **Step 3 — Vary Load:** Apply a step-wise mechanical/electrical brake load using a **rope-brake dynamometer** (older labs) or an **eddy-current dynamometer** (modern labs).
- **Step 4 — Record Observations:** For each load step, record $Q$ (via venturimeter/orifice meter), $N$ (tachometer), $W$ (spring balance reading on dynamometer), and gauge readings.
- **Step 5 — Compute & Plot:** Use the formulas in §2.3 to compute Power, Efficiency, etc., and plot the characteristic curves.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear in nearly every KTU Numerical, Part A, and Part B question on this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discharge through venturimeter | $Q = C_d \cdot a_1 a_2 \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $h$ = manometer difference in metres of water |
| 2 | Water (Hydraulic) Power | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | W (or kW ÷ 1000) | $H$ = net head in metres |
| 3 | Shaft (Brake) Power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | $T$ = torque in N·m |
| 4 | Torque from Rope-Brake | $T = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g$ | N·m | $W$ = load, $S$ = spring balance, $R$ = brake wheel radius |
| 5 | Shaft Power (Rope-Brake form) | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{(W-S) \cdot 2\pi R N}{60}$ | W | Multiplied by $g$ if weights in kgf |
| 6 | Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\rho g Q H}$ | dimensionless | The ratio to be plotted |
| 7 | Unit Discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}/\sqrt{\text{m}}$ | Used to compare turbines of different heads |
| 8 | Unit Speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm/$\sqrt{\text{m}}$ | Same purpose |
| 9 | Unit Power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | W/$\text{m}^{3/2}$ | Same purpose |
| 10 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | metric units | Used to select turbine type |
| 11 | Net Head | $H = H_{gross} - h_f$ | m | $h_f$ = friction losses in penstock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Tip for KTU Scripts:** In the formulas above, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the plain vertical bar `|` (e.g., write $\vert W - S \vert$ not $\vert W - S \vert$ in raw markdown table cells) to avoid breaking markdown table rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydroelectric Power Stations** (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar in Kerala): Performance curves dictate the **turbine setting** (height above tailrace) and the choice between Francis (medium head) and Kaplan (low head).
- **Pump-as-Turbine (PAT) selection:** Performance characteristic curves of a pump running in reverse mode are identical in shape to a turbine's, enabling cost-effective micro-hydro installations.
- **Governor Tuning:** The slope of the speed-load curve tells the governor how aggressively to adjust guide-vane opening to keep $N$ constant when load changes.

### 2.5 Significance of Iso-Efficiency Curves (Mushel Curves)

Constant-efficiency contours plotted on a Head–Discharge plane. They are crucial because:
- They allow **cavitation-free** selection of safe operating zones.
- They reveal the **flatness** of efficiency — Kaplan turbines have flatter mushel curves than Francis, making them better for variable-load conditions (typical of Kerala's monsoon-fed small hydro).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shaft Power from Rope-Brake Dynamometer

> The rope-brake dynamometer is the most common torque-measuring device in KTU's PCPEL508 lab, and deriving $P_{shaft}$ from first principles is a **guaranteed 4–6 mark question** in KTU exams.

**Setup:** A rope is wound around a brake drum (radius $R$ metres) fixed to the turbine shaft. One end of the rope carries a dead weight $W$ (kgf) and the other end is supported by a spring balance reading $S$ (kgf). The net tangential force on the drum creates a braking torque opposing rotation.

**Step 1 — Net Tangential Force on the Drum:**

When the drum rotates, friction between rope and drum transmits a tangential pull. The effective tangential force in the direction of rotation equals the difference in tensions:

$$F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g \quad [\text{N}]$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ converts the kgf readings to newtons.

> *Reasoning:* The dead weight $W$ pulls one side down. The spring balance $S$ opposes this pull on the other side. The net unbalanced force creates the braking friction that absorbs the shaft power.

**Step 2 — Braking Torque on the Shaft:**

The tangential force acts at the drum radius $R$:

$$T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \cdot g \cdot R \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

> *Reasoning:* Torque is force times perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation. Here, the radius $R$ is exactly that distance.

**Step 3 — Angular Velocity of the Shaft:**

For a shaft rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

> *Reasoning:* Each revolution is $2\pi$ radians. There are $N$ revolutions per minute, hence $N/60$ revolutions per second, hence $\omega$ radians per second.

**Step 4 — Power Absorbed by the Brake (Shaft Power):**

By the rotational form of the work-rate equation, $P = T \omega$:

$$P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega = (W - S) \cdot g \cdot R \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R g}{60} \quad [\text{W}]}$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic (Water) Power:**

The energy delivered by the water per second:

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 6 — Overall Hydraulic Efficiency:**

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R g / 60}{\rho g Q H}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_h = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60 \rho Q H}}$$

> *Reasoning:* The $g$ cancels because it appears in both numerator (force) and denominator (weight per unit volume = $\rho g$). This is the elegant cancellation the examiner loves to see in your derivation.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Model KTU Problem

**Problem:** In a performance test on a Francis turbine, the following data are recorded at constant head $H = 8$ m and speed $N = 750$ rpm:
- Discharge $Q = 0.20 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Net brake load: $W = 50$ kgf, $S = 4$ kgf
- Brake drum radius $R = 0.30$ m
- Density of water $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Calculate:** (i) Shaft Power in kW, (ii) Water Power in kW, (iii) Hydraulic Efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Net Tangential Force:**

$$F_{net} = (W - S) g = (50 - 4) \times 9.81 = 46 \times 9.81 = 451.26 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Torque:**

$$T = F_{net} \times R = 451.26 \times 0.30 = 135.378 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Angular Velocity:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 750}{60} = 78.5398 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft Power (i):**

$$P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega = 135.378 \times 78.5398 = 10{,}633.6 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{10.63 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5 — Water Power (ii):**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 \times 8 = 15{,}696 \text{ W} = \mathbf{15.70 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic Efficiency (iii):**

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{10.63}{15.70} = \mathbf{0.677 \text{ or } 67.7\%}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Francis vs. Kaplan

| Parameter | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Specific speed $N_s$ (metric) | 60 – 400 | 400 – 1000 |
| Head range | 30 m – 600 m | 2 m – 70 m |
| Flow direction through runner | Radial → Axial (mixed) | Axial throughout |
| Runner blade type | Fixed (non-adjustable) | Adjustable (swiveling) |
| Number of runner blades | 12 – 24 | 3 – 6 (propeller-like) |
| Guide vane mechanism | Wicket gate (adjustable) | Wicket gate (adjustable) |
| Efficiency curve shape | Peaked (sensitive to load) | Flat (insensitive to load) |
| Part-load efficiency | Poor (drops sharply) | Excellent (maintains high $\eta$) |
| Typical application | Idukki, Sardar Sarovar | Kerala small hydro, run-of-river |
| Cavitation susceptibility | Moderate (low setting) | High (requires low setting) |

### 3.4 Derivation of Unit Quantities

Unit quantities are non-dimensional normalizers that allow direct comparison of geometrically similar turbines operating at different heads. They arise from **dimensional homogeneity** of the governing equations.

For geometrically similar turbines operating at different heads $H_1$ and $H_2$, dimensional analysis gives:

$$\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \left(\dfrac{H_1}{H_2}\right)^{1/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{Q_1}{\sqrt{H_1}} = \dfrac{Q_2}{\sqrt{H_2}} = \text{constant} = Q_u$$

$$\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \left(\dfrac{H_1}{H_2}\right)^{1/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$$

$$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{H_1}{H_2}\right)^{3/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$$

> *Reasoning (dimensional):* $Q \propto D^2 \sqrt{2gH}$, $N \propto \sqrt{2gH}/D$, $P \propto \rho g Q H \propto D^2 H^{3/2}$. Eliminating $D$ gives the above.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Block Architecture of the Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Upper Reservoir Tank] --> B[Control Valve and Penstock Inlet]
    B --> C[Pressure Gauge at Inlet]
    C --> D[Francis or Kaplan Turbine with Wicket Gates]
    D --> E[Shaft with Tachometer and Brake Drum]
    E --> F[Rope-Brake Dynamometer with Spring Balance]
    E --> G[Tailrace with Measuring Tank and Orifice Meter]
    D --> H[Venturimeter or Orifice Meter for Q]
    F --> I[Spring Balance Reading S]
    F --> J[Dead Weight W on Rope]
    E --> K[Speed Indicator N in rpm]
    H --> L[Data Acquisition and Computation Block]
    G --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Plotting of Characteristic Curves]
    M --> N[Head vs Discharge H-Q]
    M --> O[Efficiency vs Discharge eta-Q]
    M --> P[Power vs Discharge P-Q]
    M --> Q[Speed vs Discharge N-Q]
    M --> R[Iso-Efficiency Mushel Curves on H-Q Plane]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#ffddcc,stroke:#cc3300
    style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style F fill:#e2ccff,stroke:#330099
```
