```mermaid
    style M fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

### 4.2 Logical Flow of Performance Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Start Pump and Prime Penstock] --> S2[Step 2: Adjust Valve to Set Design Head H]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Run Turbine at Synchronising Speed N]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Initial Minimum Load]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Record Q, N, W, S, h readings]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Increase Load Incrementally]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Repeat Readings up to Maximum Safe Load]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Decrease Load and Verify Repeatability]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute Pshaft, Pwater and eta for each step]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Plot Characteristic Curves]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Identify Best Efficiency Point and Safe Operating Zone]

    style S1 fill:#e6f3ff
    style S10 fill:#ffe6e6
    style S11 fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Rope-Brake Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Shaft_Assembly
        T[Turbine Shaft rotating at N rpm] --> D[Brake Drum of radius R]
    end

    D --> R1[Rope wound around drum]
    R1 --> W1[Dead Weight W hanging on one side]
    R1 --> W2[Spring Balance S on the other side]

    FT[Net Tangential Force equals W minus S times g] --> TQ[Braking Torque T equals Fnet times R]
    TQ --> PW[Shaft Power Pshaft equals T times omega]

    style T fill:#ffcc66
    style D fill:#ff9966
    style W1 fill:#ccff99
    style W2 fill:#ff99cc
    style PW fill:#99ccff
```

### 4.4 Component Inventory of the Test Rig

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Performance Test Rig for Francis Kaplan Turbines))
    Hydraulic Circuit
      Upper Tank
      Penstock with Control Valve
      Inlet Pressure Gauge
      Volumetric Tank or Orifice Meter
      Tailrace Flume
    Turbine Assembly
      Spiral Casing
      Stay Vanes
      Guide Vanes Wicket Gates
      Runner Francis or Kaplan
      Draft Tube
    Measurement Instruments
      Tachometer
      Rope Brake or Eddy Current Dynamometer
      Spring Balance
      Manometer for Venturimeter
      Bourdon Pressure Gauges
    Output Devices
      Alternator or Loading Bank
      Control Panel
      Data Logger
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*List any three differences between a Francis turbine and a Kaplan turbine.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Flow direction:** In a Francis turbine, water flows *mixed* (radial entry, axial exit) through the runner; in a Kaplan turbine, water flows *purely axially* throughout.
2. **Runner blades:** Francis runner has *fixed* blades (12–24 in number); Kaplan runner has *adjustable/swiveling* blades (3–6 propeller-like blades).
3. **Specific speed:** Francis operates in the medium range ($N_s = 60$–$400$ metric); Kaplan operates in the high range ($N_s = 400$–$1000+$ metric).
4. **Head range:** Francis is suited for medium-high heads (30–600 m); Kaplan is suited for low heads (2–70 m).
5. **Part-load efficiency:** Francis efficiency drops sharply off-design; Kaplan efficiency remains nearly flat (within 2–3%) over a wide load range.

(Any 3 differences — 1 mark each)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*What is meant by "unit discharge" and "unit speed" of a turbine? Why are they introduced?*

**Model Answer:**
- **Unit Discharge** ($Q_u$): The discharge of a turbine operating under a *unit head* (1 m), expressed as $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$. Units: $\text{m}^3/\text{s} / \sqrt{\text{m}}$.
- **Unit Speed** ($N_u$): The speed of a turbine operating under a *unit head*, expressed as $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$. Units: $\text{rpm} / \sqrt{\text{m}}$.
- **Reason for introduction:** They enable **non-dimensional comparison** of geometrically similar turbines operating at different heads and speeds. The characteristic curves of all geometrically similar turbines, when plotted against $Q_u$ and $N_u$, **coincide** into a single universal curve. This is fundamental to model testing and prototype scaling. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the procedure for conducting a performance test on a Francis turbine test rig. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Test Rig Description (2 marks):**
The test rig consists of an upper reservoir feeding a Francis turbine through a penstock. A **rope-brake dynamometer** is mounted on the turbine shaft. Discharge is measured using a **venturimeter/orifice meter** fitted in the penstock, and the tailrace water is collected in a **measuring tank**. A **tachometer** reads shaft speed. Pressure tappings at inlet and outlet of the turbine give the net head.

2. **Test Procedure (4 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Start the supply pump and prime the penstock; open the inlet valve slowly.
   - **Step 2:** Adjust the control valve to establish the **design head** ($H$) — confirmed by manometer/pressure gauge readings.
   - **Step 3:** Run the turbine at the rated **synchronising speed** ($N$) using a speed-control mechanism.
   - **Step 4:** Apply an initial **minimum load** (small dead weight $W$) on the rope brake.
   - **Step 5:** Record the readings: $W$, $S$ (spring balance), $N$ (tachometer), $h$ (venturimeter manometer), and the inlet/outlet pressure heads.
   - **Step 6:** Increase $W$ in **6 to 8 equal steps** up to the maximum safe load. At each step, allow steady-state conditions and record all readings.
   - **Step 7:** Repeat the run in *descending* order to check repeatability (hysteresis).
   - **Step 8:** Reduce the load to zero, then shut down the rig safely.

3. **Precautions (1 mark):**
   - Never exceed the rated head or speed (cavitation / mechanical damage).
   - Ensure the spring balance reads below the dead weight (otherwise the brake is ineffective).
   - Wait for steady state before recording (typically 1–2 minutes per step).

---

**(b) The following data were obtained during a performance test on a Francis turbine at a constant head of 10 m and constant speed of 600 rpm. The brake drum diameter is 0.6 m. The spring balance reading is 5 kgf at no-load. Compute the shaft power, water power, and efficiency for each load, and plot the characteristic curves. (7 marks)**

| Load $W$ (kgf) | 30 | 35 | 40 | 45 | 50 | 55 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge $Q$ (L/s) | 180 | 200 | 220 | 240 | 260 | 280 |

**Model Solution:**

Using $R = 0.30$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $H = 10$ m, $N = 600$ rpm.

Angular velocity: $\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 62.832$ rad/s.

For each load, compute $T = (W - 5) \times 9.81 \times 0.30$ and $P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega$.

**Sample Calculation for $W = 30$ kgf:**

$$F_{net} = (30 - 5) \times 9.81 = 245.25 \text{ N}$$

$$T = 245.25 \times 0.30 = 73.575 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 73.575 \times 62.832 = 4623 \text{ W} = 4.62 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.180 \times 10 = 17{,}658 \text{ W} = 17.66 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_h = 4.62 / 17.66 = 0.262 = 26.2\%$$

**Full Computed Table (for all 6 loads):**

| $W$ (kgf) | $W-S$ (kgf) | $T$ (N·m) | $P_{shaft}$ (kW) | $Q$ (L/s) | $P_{water}$ (kW) | $\eta_h$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30 | 25 | 73.58 | 4.62 | 180 | 17.66 | 26.2 |
| 35 | 30 | 88.29 | 5.55 | 200 | 19.62 | 28.3 |
| 40 | 35 | 103.0 | 6.47 | 220 | 21.58 | 30.0 |
| 45 | 40 | 117.7 | 7.40 | 240 | 23.54 | 31.4 |
| 50 | 45 | 132.4 | 8.32 | 260 | 25.51 | 32.6 |
| 55 | 50 | 147.2 | 9.25 | 280 | 27.47 | 33.7 |

**Plotting Instructions (1 mark):**
- Plot $H$ vs $Q$ (here $H$ is constant — a horizontal line).
- Plot $\eta_h$ vs $Q$ (rises to a peak then falls — the *Best Efficiency Point* is near $Q = 280$ L/s here, $\eta \approx 33.7\%$).
- Plot $P_{shaft}$ vs $Q$ (rises monotonically).
- Optionally, plot **iso-efficiency curves** on a Head–Discharge plane for design selection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the no-load spring-balance correction:** The spring balance reads $S_0$ even at zero load due to rope mass. Always use $W - S$ where both are *simultaneous* readings. If $S_0$ at no-load is 5 kgf, this offset must be subtracted.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $Q$ in the table is in L/s — convert to m³/s by dividing by 1000 before computing $P_{water}$. Forgetting this introduces a 1000× error and costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Dropping the factor of 2 in the rope-brake formula:** Some texts use $(W - S) \cdot R$ for torque (single-side) while the KTU-conventional formula already accounts for the effective tangential pull as $(W - S) \cdot g \cdot R$. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Not identifying the BEP:** The question often asks you to "identify the best efficiency point" — explicitly state the coordinates $(Q_{BEP}, \eta_{max})$ from your plot.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Define "specific speed" of a turbine. How is it used to classify Francis and Kaplan turbines? Compute the specific speed of a turbine developing 500 kW under a head of 25 m at 400 rpm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The specific speed ($N_s$) of a turbine is the speed at which a *geometrically similar* turbine would run while developing **unit power (1 kW) under unit head (1 m)**. It is a *non-dimensional index* used to classify reaction turbines by their geometry and flow pattern.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

where $N$ is in rpm, $P$ in kW, and $H$ in metres (metric units).

**Classification (2 marks):**
- $N_s = 8$ to $30$ → Pelton wheel
- $N_s = 30$ to $60$ → Francis (slow)
- $N_s = 60$ to $400$ → Francis (fast/normal)
- $N_s = 400$ to $1000+$ → Kaplan / Propeller

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$N_s = \dfrac{400 \times \sqrt{500}}{25^{5/4}} = \dfrac{400 \times 22.361}{25^{1.25}}$$

Computing $25^{1.25} = 25 \times 25^{0.25} = 25 \times \sqrt{5} = 25 \times 2.236 = 55.90$

$$N_s = \dfrac{8944.3}{55.90} \approx \mathbf{160}$$

Since $N_s = 160$ falls in the 60–400 range → **Francis turbine (fast type)**.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of iso-efficiency (Mushel) curves. With reference to a Kaplan turbine, describe why these curves are flatter than those of a Francis turbine. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Iso-efficiency curves, also called **Mushel curves** or **constant-efficiency contours**, are loci of points on the Head–Discharge ($H$–$Q$) plane at which the turbine operates at the *same* hydraulic efficiency. They are obtained by cross-plotting the constant-efficiency lines from a family of $H$–$Q$ curves generated at multiple constant speeds.

**Geometric Meaning (2 marks):** Each closed contour is centred on the Best Efficiency Point (BEP). The "spread" of these contours indicates the **range of heads and discharges** over which the turbine maintains acceptable efficiency (say, within 2% of $\eta_{max}$).

**Why Kaplan has flatter Mushel curves (3 marks):**
- Kaplan turbines have **adjustable runner blades** that can swivel to match the incoming flow angle at off-design conditions. The runner geometry *adapts* to the flow, maintaining high efficiency.
- Francis turbines have **fixed runner blades**. When the operating point moves away from the BEP, the flow angle at runner inlet deviates from the design blade angle, creating **shock losses** and a sharp drop in efficiency.
- Hence, the **iso-efficiency contours** of a Kaplan turbine are **widely spaced and nearly parallel to the $Q$-axis** (efficiency is largely insensitive to $Q$ for a given $H$). Francis contours are **narrower and elliptical**, indicating a sharply peaked efficiency region.

This is why Kaplan is preferred for **variable load** conditions (typical of small hydro in Kerala, where monsoon flow is highly seasonal), while Francis is preferred for **base-load, near-constant load** operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — "Mushel" vs "Muschel":** Both spellings appear in textbooks. The correct German origin is "Muschelkurven" (literally "mussel curves" because of the shape). Use "Mushel" in KTU answers — it's the form the official answer key uses.
>
> **Second Pitfall:** A common error is to confuse **specific speed** ($N_s$) with **unit speed** ($N_u$). Remember: $N_s$ is for *classification*, $N_u$ is for *comparison of similar turbines*. Do not interchange them in viva or written answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day KTU prep):**

- ✅ **Reaction turbines** (Francis, Kaplan) vs **impulse turbines** (Pelton) — pressure drop across the runner.
- ✅ **Francis** = mixed flow, fixed blades, $N_s$ 60–400, medium heads; **Kaplan** = axial flow, adjustable blades, $N_s$ 400–1000+, low heads.
- ✅ **Rope-brake shaft power** formula: $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N (W - S) R g / 60$. Always subtract the *no-load* spring balance reading.
- ✅ **Hydraulic efficiency** = $P_{shaft} / (\rho g Q H)$ — the only efficiency computed in the KTU lab test (mechanical and volumetric losses are usually neglected at the syllabus level).
- ✅ **Unit quantities:** $Q_u = Q/\sqrt{H}$, $N_u = N/\sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P/H^{3/2}$. Use them to **plot dimensionless characteristic curves** that are valid for all geometrically similar turbines.
- ✅ **Specific speed** $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ is a *classification index* — higher $N_s$ means higher discharge, lower head, axial-flow design.
- ✅ **Mushel (iso-efficiency) curves:** Plotted on the $H$–$Q$ plane. They are **flatter for Kaplan** (good for variable load) and **narrower for Francis**.
- ✅ **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Coordinates $(Q_{BEP}, \eta_{max}, H_{design})$ — always identify and tabulate.
- ✅ **Constant head, constant speed** are the two fixed conditions in this test. Load is the independent variable.
- ✅ **Net head** $H = H_{gross} - h_f$ — do *not* confuse with the static tank level difference; friction losses in the penstock must be subtracted if pressure tappings are at the tank and turbine inlet.
- ✅ **Runaway condition:** If load is suddenly removed, the turbine accelerates to a very high "runaway speed" that can damage the runner. Always reduce load *gradually*.
- ✅ **Cavitation check:** Specific speed > 350 in metric units is a warning sign — Kaplan installations must have a low **setting height** (turbine elevation above tailrace) to suppress cavitation.
- ✅ **Common conversions:** $1$ kgf = $9.81$ N; $1$ L/s = $10^{-3}$ m³/s; $1$ kW = $1000$ W.
- ✅ **KTU answer-writing tip:** When a numerical involves $g$ in both numerator and denominator (as in the rope-brake efficiency derivation), explicitly *show the cancellation* in your solution — the examiner awards marks for that algebraic clarity.
- ✅ **Syllabus-aligned experiment objectives** (PCPEL508): (1) Determine characteristic curves, (2) Compute efficiency, (3) Identify BEP, (4) Plot iso-efficiency curves, (5) Compare Francis and Kaplan performance.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Performance Testing of Francis/Kaplan Turbines | PCPEL508 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristics of Single and Multi-Stage Centrifugal Pumps
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Performance Characteristics of Single and Multi-Stage Centrifugal Pumps

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL508 - PUMPS AND PRIME MOVERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics of Single and Multi-Stage Centrifugal Pumps

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

A **Centrifugal Pump** is a **rotodynamic machine** that converts the **mechanical energy** of a prime mover (electric motor/IC engine) into **hydraulic energy** (pressure + kinetic) by imparting **centrifugal force** to the fluid through a rotating **impeller**. The **performance characteristics** describe the functional relationship between the **head (H)**, **discharge (Q)**, **shaft power (SP)**, and **efficiency (η)** of the pump under varying operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A centrifugal pump's **performance curve** is a graphical representation obtained experimentally by varying the discharge through a delivery valve, plotting the head developed, power consumed, and overall efficiency against the volumetric flow rate.

**Performance Characteristic (PC)** of a centrifugal pump = the set of four inter-related curves:
1. **H vs Q** (Head–Discharge)
2. **SP vs Q** (Shaft Power–Discharge)
3. **η vs Q** (Efficiency–Discharge)
4. **NPSH vs Q** (Net Positive Suction Head–Discharge, secondary)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🪣 The Bucket-and-Swing Analogy**
> Imagine you are holding a bucket of water tied to a rope and swinging it around in a circle. The faster you swing (higher RPM), the more the water tries to fly outward (centrifugal effect). Now, the water exits the bucket and hits the side of a circular tub. The bucket = **impeller**, the swinging action = **rotation**, the water flying out = **kinetic energy**, and the water hitting the tub wall = **pressure rise**.
> - A **single-stage pump** has ONE bucket swing (one impeller).
> - A **multi-stage pump** has MULTIPLE bucket swings in series (multiple impellers on one shaft) → more pressure built up progressively, like climbing stairs one at a time.

### 1.3 Single-Stage vs Multi-Stage — KTU Distinction

| Feature | Single-Stage Centrifugal Pump | Multi-Stage Centrifugal Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Impellers** | One | Two or more (in series) |
| **Head Range** | Low to medium (10–100 m) | High (50–500+ m) |
| **Discharge** | Higher (radial/axial flow) | Lower per stage (added) |
| **Pressure Rise per Stage** | Limited by one impeller | Cumulative across stages |
| **Typical Application** | Water supply, HVAC, irrigation | Boiler feed, high-rise pumping, oil pipelines |
| **Construction** | Compact, volute/single casing | Modular — stages stacked on common shaft |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (precision alignment) |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Specific Gravity (SG)** of water at standard conditions = **1.0**
- **Density of water (ρ)** = **1000 kg/m³ = 1 kg/L**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard test temperature** = **27 °C (300 K)**
- **Rated Speed (N)** = constant for a given test; in KTU lab, typically **2900 RPM** or **1450 RPM**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Standard:** The characteristic curve is plotted at **constant rated speed (N = constant)**. If speed is varied, the affinity (pump) laws are applied to predict the new performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The performance of a centrifugal pump is governed by the **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**, which relates the energy transfer to the impeller blade velocities.

### Step 1: Energy Conversion Stages
1. **Mechanical → Kinetic:** The impeller imparts velocity to fluid (centrifugal acceleration).
2. **Kinetic → Pressure:** Fluid exits into the volute/diffuser, where kinetic energy is **decelerated** and converted to **pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle).
3. **Total Head (H):** Sum of **static head + velocity head + friction losses** at the delivery point minus suction side energy.

### Step 2: Why is the H–Q Curve Downward Sloping?
- As delivery valve opens → discharge (Q) **increases**.
- Due to higher flow velocities, **friction losses** (cubic in Q) and **circulation losses** increase.
- Therefore, the **developed head (H)** falls with rising Q.
- This gives the characteristic **monotonically decreasing** H–Q curve.

### Step 3: Why is the SP–Q Curve Slightly Upward?
- At shut-off (Q = 0), the impeller just churns the water → some power still consumed → **SP is not zero** at Q = 0.
- As Q increases, friction and hydraulic losses increase → **SP rises** with Q.

### Step 4: Why is the η–Q Curve Bell-Shaped?
- At Q = 0 → η = 0 (no useful work delivered).
- At very high Q → η drops (losses dominate).
- **Maximum efficiency (η_max)** occurs at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** — the design operating condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Designers always select pumps to operate near the BEP (typically 70–110% of BEP flow) for optimal energy consumption and minimum wear.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Manometric Head (Hm)** | $H_m = H_s + H_d + \dfrac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m of liquid | Suction + Delivery gauge heads + velocity heads |
| 2 | **Input Power (P_i)** | $P_i = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | N = RPM, T = Torque in N·m |
| 3 | **Output (Water) Power (P_o)** | $P_o = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_m$ | W | $\rho$ in kg/m³, Q in m³/s, H in m |
| 4 | **Overall Efficiency (η_o)** | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_o}{P_i} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{2\pi N T / 60}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | Key performance metric |
| 5 | **Manometric Efficiency (η_man)** | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}$ | dimensionless | Euler's head / Euler's velocity term |
| 6 | **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{Water Power}}{\text{Shaft Power}}$ | dimensionless | Bearing/friction loss factor |
| 7 | **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | Accounts for internal leakage |
| 8 | **Specific Speed (N_s)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | dimensionless (SI) | Determines pump type (radial/mixed/axial) |
| 9 | **Affinity Law — Head** | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | — | At constant impeller diameter |
| 10 | **Affinity Law — Discharge** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | — | Linear with speed |
| 11 | **Affinity Law — Power** | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | — | Cubic with speed |
| 12 | **NPSH (Available)** | $NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_{vapor} - H_s - h_{fs}$ | m | Must exceed NPSH_required |
| 13 | **Shut-off Head Multi-Stage** | $H_{total} = H_{stage} \times n$ | m | n = number of stages |
| 14 | **Discharge from Measuring Tank** | $Q = \dfrac{A_{tank} \times h}{t}$ | m³/s | Volumetric method (preferred in KTU lab) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the lab exam, students are **expected** to know the difference between *Manometric Head* (the head actually imparted to water) and *Total Head* (includes suction-side velocity correction). Most marks are lost here.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BEP selection** prevents **cavitation**, **vibration**, and **premature bearing failure** in industrial plants.
- **Affinity laws** allow prediction of new performance when an impeller is trimmed or speed is changed with a VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) — critical for **energy audits**.
- **Specific Speed** classifies pumps for standardized applications (radial: $N_s$ < 500, mixed: 500–4000, axial: > 4000).
- **Multi-stage pumps** are essential in **boiler feed systems** (thermal power plants) where steam generation pressures of **>100 bar** demand discharge heads exceeding **1500 m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Manometric Head from Bernoulli's Equation

Applying the energy equation between the **suction tank free surface (1)** and the **delivery tank free surface (2)** through the pump, including all head losses:

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_m = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{losses}
$$

**Step 1:** At both free surfaces, $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ (gauge pressure = 0) and $V_1 \approx V_2 \approx 0$ (large tank area).

**Step 2:** The remaining terms simplify:

$$
H_m = (z_2 - z_1) + h_{losses}
$$

**Step 3:** Defining the **static suction head (Hs)** as the height of pump centerline below suction tank water level, and **static delivery head (Hd)** as the height above delivery tank water level, with $h_{ls}$ and $h_{ld}$ being suction and delivery pipe friction losses:

$$
H_m = H_d + H_s + h_{ls} + h_{ld}
$$

**Step 4:** When **velocity heads** at the pump suction ($V_s$) and delivery ($V_d$) points are not negligible (especially in lab setups with small pipes):

$$
H_m = H_s + H_d + \dfrac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}
$$

This is the **KTU-recommended manometric head formula** used in the lab calculation table.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Efficiency (Water Power / Shaft Power)

**Step 1:** Water Power (Output) — from the definition of hydraulic power:

$$
P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_m \quad [\text{W}]
$$

where $\rho g H_m$ is the **pressure** imparted per unit volume, and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate.

**Step 2:** Shaft (Brake) Power (Input) — derived from torque and angular speed:

$$
T = F \times r \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 3:** Combining:

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_m}{2\pi N T / 60}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting numerical values for water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²) and simplifying:

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times Q \times H_m \times 60}{2\pi N T} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 30000 \cdot Q \cdot H_m}{2\pi N T}
$$

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{294300 \cdot Q \cdot H_m}{\pi N T} \quad \text{(multiplied by 100 for percentage)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Calculation Rule:** Always express Q in m³/s (NOT L/s or LPM) and H in meters, before substituting into the efficiency formula. A common student error is forgetting to convert.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Affinity (Pump) Laws

These laws are derived from the **similarity principle** in turbomachinery: two geometrically similar pumps operating at different speeds share **kinematic and dynamic similarity**.

**Step 1:** Discharge is proportional to impeller tangential velocity (since $Q \propto V \times A$ and $A$ is constant for geometric similarity):

$$
\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

**Step 2:** Head is proportional to square of velocity (from Euler's equation, $H \propto V^2 / g$):

$$
\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Power is proportional to cube of velocity (since $P = \rho g Q H$):

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** If a pump rated 1500 RPM delivers 100 m head and consumes 10 kW, then at 1800 RPM (1.2× speed), the new head = 144 m, and new power = 17.28 kW. This cubic relationship is why **VFD speed reduction** saves enormous energy in partial-load operation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Stage Pump Performance

For a multi-stage pump with $n$ identical stages in series, assuming **equal inter-stage discharge** and **negligible inter-stage losses**:

$$
H_{total} = n \cdot H_{single\ stage}
$$

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{single\ stage} \quad (\text{flow passes through all stages})
$$

$$
P_{shaft,total} = n \cdot P_{shaft,single\ stage}
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{stage} \quad (\text{approximately, per stage})
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**

A 3-stage centrifugal pump has each stage delivering 25 m head at 50 L/s with stage efficiency 75%. Find total head, discharge, input power.

**Solution:**

$$
H_{total} = 3 \times 25 = 75 \text{ m}
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 50 \text{ L/s} = 0.050 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.050 \times 75 = 36,787.5 \text{ W} = 36.79 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{36.79}{0.75} = 49.05 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** If the question specifies the pump as a **parallel multi-stage** arrangement (rare, but possible for low-head high-flow), then $Q_{total} = n \cdot Q_{single}$ and $H_{total} = H_{single}$. Always read the problem statement carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — KTU Centrifugal Pump Test Rig (Experimental Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suction Tank with Water] --> B[Foot Valve and Strainer]
    B --> C[Suction Pipe with Vacuum Gauge]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Pump Unit]
    D --> E[Delivery Pipe with Pressure Gauge]
    E --> F[Delivery Valve GV]
    F --> G[Measuring Tank with Level Indicator]
    G --> H[Stopwatch and Stopcock]
    D --> I[Dynamometer / Torque Sensor]
    I --> J[Variable Speed Drive or Tachometer]
    J --> K[3-Phase Electric Motor M]
    K --> D
    H --> L[Data Acquisition and Tabulation]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    L --> M[Calculation of H Q SP eta]
    M --> N[Plotting Characteristic Curves H vs Q SP vs Q eta vs Q]
```

**Component Description (Block-Level Architecture Flow):**

| Block | Component | KTU Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Suction Tank | Reservoir providing fluid; level must be ≥ pump inlet |
| B | Foot Valve + Strainer | Prevents back-flow and filters debris |
| C | Vacuum Gauge | Measures suction-side vacuum (used to compute Hs) |
| D | Centrifugal Pump Unit | Test specimen (single or multi-stage) |
| E | Pressure Gauge | Measures delivery pressure (used to compute Hd) |
| F | Delivery Gate Valve | Used to **throttle** and vary Q for plotting curves |
| G | Measuring Tank | Calibrated tank (e.g., 0.5 m × 0.5 m base) to find Q |
| H | Stopwatch | Time collected water for Q = Area × Rise / time |
| I | Dynamometer / Load Cell | Measures braking torque for shaft power |
| J | Tachometer | Confirms rotational speed (N in RPM) |
| K | Electric Motor | Prime mover (3-phase induction motor preferred) |
| L | Data Table | Manual logging of (Q, Hs, Hd, N, T) for each setting |
| M | Calculation Engine | Applies Hm, SP, η formulas |
| N | Characteristic Plot | Final H, SP, η vs Q curves |

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Data Acquisition to Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Test] --> S2[Open Delivery Valve Fully]
    S2 --> S3[Record No-Load Data Q0 H0 SP0]
    S3 --> S4[Throttle Valve Partially Closed]
    S4 --> S5[Record Data at New Q1]
    S5 --> S6{More Throttle Settings?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S4
    S6 -->|No| S7[Compile All Data Sets]
    S7 --> S8[Calculate Hm SP eta for Each Set]
    S8 --> S9[Tabulate Q Hm SP eta]
    S9 --> S10[Plot H vs Q]
    S9 --> S11[Plot SP vs Q]
    S9 --> S12[Plot eta vs Q]
    S10 --> S13[Identify BEP]
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S13
    S13 --> S14[Report Performance Characteristics]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Multi-Stage Pump Flow Path (Series Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Impeller] --> P2[Inter-stage Guide Vanes]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 2 Impeller]
    P3 --> P4[Inter-stage Guide Vanes]
    P4 --> P5[Stage N Impeller]
    P5 --> P6[Volute Casing Collector]
    P6 --> P7[Delivery Nozzle]
    P0[Suction Inlet] --> P1
    P7 --> P8[To Delivery Pipe]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a multi-stage centrifugal pump, the fluid enters the **first-stage impeller eye** at the suction, gains energy, is redirected by **return guide vanes** into the **second-stage impeller eye**, and the process repeats. The volute at the end collects fluid from the final stage.
